name: Labrador Retriever
common_training_challenges: Pulling on leash due to strength
training_tips: Highly food-motivated — use kibble from daily meals as training treats to avoid overfeeding.
Mouthy breed: keep redirect toys handy for the first 6 months. Bite inhibition is critical.
Channel retrieval instinct into training games. Fetch is both exercise and training.
Impulse control is the #1 skill to prioritize — Labs are enthusiastic, which is great but needs structure.
Water-loving breed. Introduce water early and use swim sessions as high-value rewards.
recommended_training_style: Food-motivated, high-energy sessions, retrieval-based games, positive reinforcement

common_conditions.description: Abnormal development of the hip joint causing pain and lameness.
Developmental condition of the elbow joint.
Degenerative eye disease leading to vision loss.
Genetic condition causing muscle weakness during intense exercise.
Labs are genetically predisposed to overeating due to a POMC gene mutation.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 12-18 months for large breed joint development. Discuss timing with your vet.
breed_description:
The Labrador Retriever is America's most popular dog breed for good reason. Originally bred as fishing dogs in Newfoundland, Labs combine athleticism, intelligence, and an unshakeable love of people into one enthusiastic package.

Labs are the quintessential family dog — patient with children, friendly with strangers, and eager to please their owners. Their food motivation makes them exceptionally trainable, though their boundless energy requires consistent outlets.

As puppies, expect a mouthy, energetic whirlwind for the first 18 months. The teenage phase (6-18 months) can be challenging as their size outpaces their impulse control. With consistent training, they mature into the loyal, gentle companions the breed is famous for.
history: Developed in 19th-century Newfoundland (not Labrador) as water dogs for fishermen, retrieving nets and fish. British sportsmen imported them to England in the 1800s, refining the breed for game retrieval. Recognised by the AKC in 1917, they've been America's #1 breed for over 30 years.
fun_facts: Labs have a genetic mutation (POMC gene) that makes them feel perpetually hungry — it's not just greediness!
Their otter-like tail acts as a rudder when swimming.
The breed originated in Newfoundland, not Labrador — the name was a geographical mix-up.
Labs are the most common guide dog breed worldwide.
puppy_tips: Start bite inhibition training from day one — Lab puppies are very mouthy.
Manage food intake carefully. Lab puppies will eat until they're sick if given the chance.
Begin leash training early while they're still small enough to manage.
Socialise extensively between 8-16 weeks — Labs are naturally social but need positive experiences.
Invest in heavy-duty chew toys. Labs are power chewers, especially during teething.
diet_notes: Prone to obesity. Use measured meals, not free feeding. Reduce treats to 10% of daily calories. Consider slow feeder bowls to prevent gulping.
exercise_notes: High-energy breed needing 60-120 minutes daily. Swimming is ideal exercise. Avoid high-impact exercise (long runs, repetitive jumping) before 18 months to protect growing joints.
grooming_notes: Short double coat sheds heavily, especially in spring and autumn. Weekly brushing minimises shedding. Occasional baths. Check and clean ears regularly — floppy ears are prone to infection.

name: French Bulldog
training_tips: Keep sessions under 5 minutes — Frenchies overheat and lose focus quickly.
High-value treats are essential. This breed won't work for kibble alone.
Avoid outdoor training in heat. Early morning or evening sessions only in warm weather.
Stubbornness isn't defiance — it's independence. Make training fun, not repetitive.
Crate training is critical due to high separation anxiety risk. Start very gradually.
recommended_training_style: Short, fun sessions with high-value rewards. Avoid repetition. Keep it playful.

common_conditions.description: Breathing difficulties due to shortened skull and narrowed airways.
Spinal disc problems common in breeds with compact builds.
Frenchies are prone to environmental and food allergies.
Prolapse of the third eyelid gland.
symptoms: Red mass in corner of eye

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months) is usually appropriate for this breed. Discuss with your vet.
breed_description:
The French Bulldog is a compact, muscular companion with bat-like ears and a personality that far exceeds their small frame. Bred as miniature bulldogs in England and refined in France, Frenchies have become one of the world's most popular breeds.

They're ideal apartment dogs — low exercise needs, minimal barking, and maximum cuddle potential. But don't let the couch-potato reputation fool you: Frenchies are playful, alert, and surprisingly athletic in short bursts.

The breed's brachycephalic (flat-faced) structure means extra health considerations. Heat management is critical, exercise must be moderated, and breathing difficulties are common. Despite these challenges, their affectionate, comical nature makes them irresistible companions.
history: Originally bred as miniature bulldogs in England during the Industrial Revolution. Lace workers emigrating to France brought the dogs along, where they became fashionable companions in Parisian cafés. The distinctive bat ears were standardised in America. AKC recognised in 1898.
fun_facts: French Bulldogs can't swim due to their front-heavy build — always use a life jacket near water.
They were favourites of Parisian café society in the 1800s.
Most Frenchies are born via C-section due to their large heads and narrow hips.
They communicate with a unique repertoire of yawns, yips, and gargles.
puppy_tips: Monitor breathing from day one. Learn what's normal for your Frenchie's breathing sounds.
Never exercise in heat — early morning and evening only during warm months.
Clean facial folds daily to prevent skin infections.
Socialise carefully — Frenchies can be dog-selective. Positive experiences with calm dogs are key.
Start separation training immediately. Frenchies bond intensely and anxiety develops fast.
diet_notes: Prone to food allergies and obesity. Consider limited-ingredient diets. Avoid overfeeding — extra weight worsens breathing issues. Use puzzle feeders for mental stimulation.
exercise_notes: 30-60 minutes daily of moderate activity. Short walks, indoor play, and mental games are ideal. Avoid intense exercise, especially in heat. Swimming is dangerous without a life jacket.
grooming_notes: Minimal coat care — weekly brushing. Daily facial fold cleaning is essential to prevent yeast and bacterial infections. Regular ear cleaning. Nail trims every 2-3 weeks.

name: Golden Retriever
coat_type: Double coat, medium to long, wavy
training_tips: Goldens are people-pleasers — they thrive on praise as much as treats.
Channel the carrying instinct: teach 'hold' and 'drop it' early. They'll carry shoes, socks, and toys regardless.
Mouthy as puppies but soft-mouthed by nature. Bite inhibition usually progresses faster than other breeds.
Teach a solid greeting protocol early — their friendliness becomes problematic at 70+ lbs if they jump.
Retrieval games double as training and exercise. Use fetch to teach impulse control (wait before chasing).
recommended_training_style: Praise-driven, gentle corrections, retrieval-based learning, consistent routines

common_conditions.description: Goldens have a significantly higher cancer rate than most breeds, particularly hemangiosarcoma and lymphoma.
Malformation of the hip joint causing progressive arthritis.
Developmental abnormalities of the elbow joint.
Skin and food allergies are common in the breed.
Narrowing below the aortic valve restricting blood flow.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 12-18 months for joint development. Studies suggest intact Goldens may have lower cancer risk, but discuss specifics with your vet.
breed_description:
The Golden Retriever is the gold standard for family dogs — intelligent, gentle, and endlessly devoted. Originally bred for retrieving game in the Scottish Highlands, Goldens combine athleticism with a soft temperament that makes them exceptional companions, therapy dogs, and service animals.

Goldens are natural people-pleasers who learn quickly and forgive easily. Their patience with children is legendary, and their enthusiasm for life is contagious. They'll carry your shoes, greet every stranger as a long-lost friend, and look at you with those soulful eyes that make it impossible to stay annoyed.

Be aware that Goldens mature slowly — mentally, they're puppies until about 2-3 years old. The teenage phase (6-18 months) combines full adult size with puppy brain. Consistent training through this period pays enormous dividends.
history: Developed by Lord Tweedmouth in the Scottish Highlands during the 1860s by crossing a Yellow Retriever with the now-extinct Tweed Water Spaniel. Bred for retrieving waterfowl in challenging Highland terrain. AKC recognised in 1925. Third most popular breed in America.
fun_facts: Golden Retrievers have an incredibly soft mouth — they can carry a raw egg without cracking it.
They were originally called 'Golden Flat-Coats' before getting their own breed recognition.
Goldens hold multiple Guinness World Records, including loudest bark (113.1 dB).
Presidents Ford and Reagan both had Golden Retrievers in the White House.
puppy_tips: Start teaching 'drop it' immediately — Goldens carry everything and some items are dangerous.
Socialise heavily but don't assume friendliness replaces training. Friendly + untrained = chaos at 70 lbs.
Brush daily during teething — it gets them used to grooming before the full adult coat arrives.
Mental stimulation is as important as physical exercise. Puzzle toys, nose work, and training sessions prevent boredom.
Introduce grooming routines early: brushing, nail trims, ear cleaning. Their adult coat requires significant maintenance.
diet_notes: Active breed needing high-quality protein. Monitor weight carefully — Goldens can overeat. Joint supplements (glucosamine, fish oil) are beneficial from an early age.
exercise_notes: 60-120 minutes daily of active exercise. Swimming is ideal and easy on joints. Avoid repetitive high-impact exercise before 18 months. Mental stimulation equally important.
grooming_notes: Heavy shedding year-round, extreme during seasonal blows. Brush 2-3x weekly minimum, daily during shedding season. Regular ear cleaning. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks recommended.

name: German Shepherd
training_tips: Early socialisation is absolutely critical — undersocialised GSDs can become reactive or fearful.
Channel herding instinct into structured tasks. Obedience, nose work, and agility are ideal outlets.
Train impulse control around movement — bikes, joggers, and running children trigger herding drive.
Build confidence through positive exposure, not flooding. A nervous GSD is a reactive GSD.
This breed needs a job. A bored German Shepherd will create their own entertainment, and you won't like it.
recommended_training_style: Structured, consistent, task-oriented. Thrives on having a job to do.

common_conditions.description: One of the most affected breeds for hip joint malformation.
Developmental elbow joint condition causing lameness.
Progressive spinal cord disease causing hind limb weakness.
Life-threatening stomach twisting requiring emergency surgery.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 18-24 months for large breed joint development. Discuss with your vet.
breed_description:
The German Shepherd is the world's leading police, military, and service dog — and for good reason. This breed combines intelligence, loyalty, and versatility unlike any other. Originally developed for herding sheep, GSDs have become the gold standard for working dogs.

At home, a well-socialised German Shepherd is a devoted family guardian — gentle with children they're raised with, watchful but not aggressive, and deeply bonded to their people. They're one-family dogs who take their protective role seriously.

The flip side of that intelligence is a dog that needs purpose. Without mental stimulation and structured training, GSDs become anxious, destructive, or reactive. This is not a breed for passive owners — they need an engaged, consistent leader who provides both structure and affection.
history: Developed by Captain Max von Stephanitz in Germany in 1899, who wanted to create the ideal herding dog. The breed quickly proved valuable for police and military work. After World War I, returning soldiers brought GSDs to America. AKC recognised since 1908.
fun_facts: German Shepherds are the third most intelligent dog breed, behind Border Collies and Poodles.
Rin Tin Tin, a GSD rescued from a WWI battlefield, starred in 27 Hollywood films.
They can learn a new command in fewer than 5 repetitions.
GSDs have 225 million scent receptors — 45x more than humans.
puppy_tips: Socialise relentlessly between 8-16 weeks. Positive exposure to strangers, dogs, sounds, and environments is non-negotiable.
Start training basic obedience immediately — GSDs learn fast and you want to build good habits before bad ones form.
Manage nipping at heels early. Redirect herding behaviour to toys, not ankles.
Introduce handling exercises (ears, paws, mouth) early for vet visits.
Provide mental enrichment daily: puzzle toys, training sessions, nose work. A bored GSD puppy is a destructive one.
diet_notes: High-quality protein diet. Prone to digestive issues — consider easily digestible formulas. Avoid overfeeding to reduce bloat risk. Feed 2-3 smaller meals rather than one large meal.
exercise_notes: 60-120 minutes daily of both physical and mental exercise. Hiking, tracking, agility, and obedience training are ideal. Not a breed for just backyard play — they need structured activity.
grooming_notes: Heavy shedding year-round. Daily brushing during seasonal coat blows. Weekly brushing otherwise. Bathe only as needed — overbathing strips natural coat oils.

name: Poodle
common_training_challenges: Can be sensitive to harsh corrections
May become anxious without mental stimulation
Can be aloof with strangers if undersocialised
training_tips: Poodles are one of the smartest breeds — they learn incredibly fast but also learn bad habits fast.
Use variety in training. Poodles bore easily with repetitive drills.
Gentle, positive methods only. Harsh corrections damage the Poodle's sensitive temperament.
Socialise with confidence-building exercises. Poodles can be reserved with strangers if not exposed early.
Mental stimulation is as critical as physical exercise. Puzzle toys, trick training, and nose work are essential.
recommended_training_style: Positive reinforcement, variety-rich sessions, gentle handling, mental challenge emphasis

common_conditions.description: Joint malformation more common in Standard Poodles.
Degenerative eye condition leading to blindness.
Stomach twisting, especially in Standard Poodles.
Inflammatory skin disease affecting oil glands.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard: consider waiting 12-18 months. Miniature/Toy: standard 6-12 month timing usually appropriate.
breed_description:
The Poodle is far more than a pretty face. Beneath that iconic coat lies one of the most intelligent, athletic, and versatile dog breeds in existence. Originally bred as water retrievers in Germany, Poodles are natural athletes, quick learners, and devoted companions.

Standard Poodles are medium-to-large dogs with genuine athleticism. Miniature and Toy varieties bring the same intelligence in smaller packages. All sizes share the breed's hallmark traits: keen intelligence, dignified bearing, and a playful streak that surprises those who see them as purely decorative.

The Poodle's hypoallergenic coat is a major draw, but it comes with a commitment — professional grooming every 4-6 weeks is non-negotiable. The payoff is a dog that doesn't shed, is remarkably trainable, and genuinely enjoys learning new things throughout their life.
history: Originally German water retrievers called 'Pudelhund' (splashing dog). The French refined the breed and made it their national dog. The distinctive clip originated as a functional trim for swimming retrievers — pom-poms protected joints in cold water. AKC recognised in 1887.
fun_facts: Poodles are the second most intelligent dog breed after Border Collies.
The elaborate show clip originated as a practical swim trim — shaved areas reduced drag, pom-poms protected joints.
Poodles were used as truffle-hunting dogs in France and England.
They're one of the oldest purebred dogs, appearing in artwork from the 15th century.
celebrity_dogs: Puce the Poodle (John Steinbeck's travel companion in Travels with Charley)
puppy_tips: Start grooming routines from day one — daily brushing, face cleaning, paw handling. The coat will become high-maintenance.
Socialise extensively. Poodles can be reserved with strangers and need positive exposure to prevent shyness.
Begin trick training early. Poodles have exceptional trick aptitude and it builds confidence and bond.
Provide varied mental challenges. Same-old training bores a Poodle. Rotate games and exercises.
Introduce water play early — Poodles are natural swimmers and it's excellent exercise.
diet_notes: High-quality protein diet appropriate for size variety. Standard Poodles benefit from bloat-prevention feeding (smaller, more frequent meals, no exercise right after eating). Monitor for food sensitivities.
exercise_notes: 60-120 minutes daily for Standard. 45-90 for Miniature. 30-60 for Toy. Swimming, retrieval games, agility, and nose work are all excellent. Mental exercise is equally important for this brilliant breed.
grooming_notes: Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks is essential. Daily brushing between grooming sessions to prevent matting. Regular ear cleaning — floppy ears trap moisture. The coat doesn't shed but continues growing, requiring consistent maintenance.

name: Bulldog
common_training_challenges: Flatulence (not a training issue, but a reality)
training_tips: Patience is everything. Bulldogs think about whether they want to comply before doing so.
Very short sessions (3-5 minutes max). They overheat and lose interest quickly.
High-value treats only. Bulldogs won't work for anything less than premium rewards.
Never train in warm weather or direct sun. Indoor, climate-controlled training is safest.
Celebrate small wins. A Bulldog doing a basic Sit on the first ask is genuinely impressive.
recommended_training_style: Patient, high-value reward-based, ultra-short sessions, indoor training preferred

common_conditions.description: Severe breathing restriction due to shortened skull.
Prolapse of the third eyelid gland.
Joint malformation exacerbated by the breed's heavy build.
Infections in facial and body wrinkles.
Spinal disc problems causing pain and mobility issues.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months). Many Bulldogs require anaesthesia considerations due to brachycephalic anatomy — discuss with vet.
breed_description:
The Bulldog is a paradox wrapped in wrinkles — simultaneously the most stubborn and the most loveable breed you'll ever meet. With their distinctive pushed-in face, rolling gait, and perpetual expression of mild indignation, Bulldogs have charmed their way into millions of homes.

Don't expect athletic achievements or lightning-fast obedience. Bulldogs move at their own pace, both physically and mentally. What they lack in speed they make up for in character — they're gentle, affectionate, and surprisingly courageous little tanks.

Health considerations are significant with this breed. Breathing difficulties, overheating, skin infections, and joint issues require proactive management. A Bulldog owner needs to be prepared for higher vet bills and daily health maintenance.
history: Originally bred for bull-baiting in England — a violent sport where dogs attacked tethered bulls. When bull-baiting was banned in 1835, breeders redirected towards companionship, selectively breeding for gentler temperaments. The modern Bulldog is dramatically different from its athletic ancestor. AKC recognised in 1886.
fun_facts: Bulldogs are the mascot of the US Marine Corps.
Most Bulldogs can't swim — their heavy front ends cause them to sink.
They were once so athletic they could take down a bull — today's Bulldogs struggle with stairs.
Bulldogs frequently travel first class on airlines due to breathing concerns in cargo holds.
puppy_tips: Monitor breathing constantly. Learn what's normal and what signals distress.
Clean facial wrinkles daily — trapped moisture causes infection rapidly.
Never exercise in heat. Bulldogs can die from overheating faster than almost any other breed.
Keep training sessions to 3-5 minutes maximum. Shorter but more frequent works best.
Invest in a good vet relationship early. Bulldogs are high-maintenance medically and you'll need it.
diet_notes: Prone to obesity and food allergies. Feed measured meals of high-quality food. Avoid fillers and common allergens. Slow feeder bowls help prevent gulping and gas.
exercise_notes: 20-40 minutes daily maximum. Short, gentle walks in cool weather. Indoor play during warm months. Never push a Bulldog past their comfort level — they will overheat before they show signs.
grooming_notes: Weekly brushing. Daily wrinkle cleaning is essential. Regular cleaning of the tail pocket (skin fold under the tail). Nail trims every 2 weeks. Ear cleaning weekly.

name: Rottweiler
coat_type: Double coat, short to medium, dense
common_training_challenges: Can be dog-reactive without proper socialisation
Mouthiness and pulling due to strength
training_tips: Socialise extensively and early. A well-socialised Rottie is confident, not aggressive.
Establish clear, consistent rules from puppyhood. This breed needs to know you're a fair leader.
Channel guarding instinct into structured obedience. 'Place' and 'Leave it' are critical commands.
Use positive reinforcement — Rotties are sensitive to harsh corrections and can become defensive.
Enrol in structured obedience classes. Group classes double as socialisation and training.
recommended_training_style: Consistent, fair leadership. Positive reinforcement with clear boundaries. Structured obedience.

common_conditions.description: Aggressive bone cancer more common in large breeds.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 18-24 months for large breed joint development.
breed_description:
The Rottweiler is a powerful, intelligent working dog whose loyalty runs deep. Originally Roman cattle drovers, Rottweilers evolved into versatile guardians, police dogs, and devoted family companions. Beneath their imposing exterior is a dog that craves connection with their people.

A well-bred, well-socialised Rottweiler is calm, confident, and surprisingly gentle with their family. They're natural guardians who don't need to be taught to protect — what they need is guidance on when protection is appropriate and when to relax.

This breed is not for everyone. They need experienced, consistent handling, extensive socialisation, and a commitment to training that lasts throughout their life. In the right home, a Rottweiler is one of the most rewarding breeds to own.
history: Descended from Roman drover dogs that accompanied cattle herds across Europe. Settled in Rottweil, Germany, where they drove cattle to market and guarded butchers' earnings. Nearly went extinct when rail replaced cattle drives, but was revived as a police and service dog. AKC recognised in 1931.
fun_facts: Rottweilers were among the earliest guide dogs for the blind.
They're one of the oldest herding breeds, descended from Roman Molossus dogs.
A Rottweiler's bite force measures around 328 PSI — one of the strongest of any breed.
They were used to pull carts for butchers in Rottweil, earning the nickname 'Butcher's Dog'.
celebrity_dogs: Apollo (fictional police dog in various media)
puppy_tips: Begin socialisation from the moment you bring them home. Positive exposure to people, dogs, and new environments is critical.
Establish training routines immediately. Rotties grow fast and behaviours set early become permanent at 100+ lbs.
Teach bite inhibition firmly but gently. Their jaw strength makes mouthy play dangerous.
Get puppy kindergarten enrolled. Group classes are essential for both obedience and socialisation.
Handle daily: paws, ears, mouth, body. A vet-friendly Rottweiler is much safer for everyone.
diet_notes: High-quality, high-protein diet for muscle maintenance. Feed 2-3 meals daily to reduce bloat risk. Monitor weight — excess weight stresses joints. Large-breed puppy formula during growth phase.
exercise_notes: 60-120 minutes daily. Hiking, swimming, obedience training, and nose work are excellent. Avoid excessive jumping or stairs during growth (under 18 months). Mental stimulation is essential.
grooming_notes: Moderate shedding. Weekly brushing with a rubber curry brush. Heavier shedding seasonally. Bathe as needed. Regular nail trims and ear cleaning.

name: Beagle
common_training_challenges: Following their nose EVERYWHERE — recall is the #1 challenge
Difficulty focusing outdoors due to scent distractions
training_tips: A Beagle's nose rules their brain. Train indoors first where scent distractions are minimal.
Recall is the biggest challenge. Use an extremely high-value reward and NEVER let them off-lead in unfenced areas until recall is bombproof.
Food motivation is your best friend. Beagles will do almost anything for food.
Teach a strong 'Leave it' early. Their nose leads them to eat things they shouldn't.
Provide scent-based enrichment: nose work, snuffle mats, scatter feeding. This satisfies their primary drive.
recommended_training_style: Food-motivated, patience with scent distractions, indoor foundations before outdoor, scent-based enrichment

common_conditions.description: Seizure disorder relatively common in Beagles.
Beagles are extremely food-motivated and prone to overeating.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months) is usually appropriate.
breed_description:
The Beagle is a nose with legs — a merry, compact hound whose world revolves around scent. Originally bred to track rabbits in packs, Beagles bring that same cheerful determination to everything they do, from investigating the garden to stealing food from the counter.

Beagles are wonderful family dogs: gentle, playful, and endlessly friendly. They adore children, get along with other dogs, and greet strangers like old friends. Their compact size and sturdy build make them adaptable to various living situations.

The challenge? That magnificent nose. When a Beagle catches an interesting scent, the rest of the world ceases to exist. Recall training is the single biggest investment a Beagle owner can make, and even then, an unfenced area is risky. Their baying — the Beagle's signature howl — is charming in small doses but can become a neighbour issue.
history: One of the oldest hound breeds, with origins tracing to ancient Greece. Refined in England for pack rabbit hunting. Small enough to follow on foot (unlike foxhounds which required horses). Brought to America in the 1860s. AKC recognised in 1885. Snoopy from Peanuts is the world's most famous Beagle.
fun_facts: Beagles have approximately 220 million scent receptors — 44x more than humans.
Snoopy from Peanuts is the world's most famous Beagle.
The US Department of Agriculture uses a 'Beagle Brigade' to sniff out prohibited food items at airports.
A Beagle's ears help funnel scent toward the nose when their head is down.
celebrity_dogs: Uno (first Beagle to win Westminster Best in Show, 2008)
puppy_tips: Start recall training immediately and make it the highest-rewarded behaviour of all.
Secure your garden completely. Beagles will dig under, squeeze through, or push past any gap if a scent leads them.
Manage food access strictly. Counter surfing starts early and a Beagle will eat until sick.
Provide daily scent enrichment: snuffle mats, scatter feeding, nose work games.
Socialise with other dogs early. Beagles are pack dogs and generally dog-friendly, but positive experiences cement this.
diet_notes: Strictly portion-controlled meals. Beagles will eat indefinitely if given the chance. Use slow feeders. Low-calorie treats for training (they need lots of treats but can't afford lots of calories). No free-feeding ever.
exercise_notes: 60-90 minutes daily. Walks with lots of sniffing time, scent games, and play. Hiking on lead is ideal. Never off-lead in unfenced areas — their nose will override any training. Scent work is the best mental exercise.
grooming_notes: Easy-care coat. Weekly brushing. Bathe as needed. Check and clean long ears regularly — floppy ears trap moisture and debris. Regular nail trims.

name: Dachshund
common_training_challenges: Potential for back injuries affecting training
training_tips: Dachshunds wrote the book on stubbornness. Make training their idea — use high-value rewards and keep it fun.
Housetraining is notoriously slow with this breed. Patience and consistency for months, not weeks.
Protect that back! No jumping on/off furniture, no stairs unsupervised, no rough play that twists the spine.
Address barking early with a solid 'Quiet' command. Dachshunds are alert barkers and it escalates without training.
Short, fun sessions only. A bored or frustrated Dachshund will simply walk away from training.
recommended_training_style: Patience-first, high-value rewards, ultra-short fun sessions, never force compliance

common_conditions.description: The #1 health risk for Dachshunds due to their long spine.
Extra weight dramatically increases IVDD risk.
Kneecap dislocation common in small breeds.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months). Discuss with vet as weight management is critical for spine health.
breed_description:
The Dachshund is a small dog with a colossal personality. Bred to hunt badgers underground (their name literally means 'badger dog'), Dachshunds combine fearless determination with a stubbornness that can be either endearing or infuriating — often both simultaneously.

Despite their comic appearance, Dachshunds are serious dogs. They're alert, vocal, and fiercely loyal to their people. They bond intensely with one person and can be reserved or even snappy with strangers if not properly socialised.

The elongated body that defines the breed is also its biggest vulnerability. Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD) is a constant concern, making weight management and spine protection the top priorities for any Dachshund owner.
history: Developed in Germany over 300 years ago to hunt badgers (Dachs = badger, Hund = dog). Their elongated body and short legs allowed them to pursue prey into underground burrows. The breed comes in two sizes (standard and miniature) and three coat types (smooth, longhaired, wirehaired). AKC recognised in 1885.
fun_facts: Dachshunds were bred to fight badgers underground — they're much braver than they look.
Picasso was so devoted to his Dachshund, Lump, that the dog appeared in many of his artworks.
They come in more coat colour and pattern combinations than almost any other breed.
During WWI, their German origins made them so unpopular in America that they were temporarily called 'liberty hounds'.
puppy_tips: Protect the spine from day one. No jumping on/off furniture — use ramps. This is non-negotiable.
Start housetraining with extreme patience. Dachshunds can take 6+ months to fully housetrain.
Socialise with strangers and other dogs early. Unsocialised Dachshunds can become snappy.
Keep weight lean. Every extra gram stresses that long spine. This is a health issue, not just cosmetic.
Begin barking management early. Teach 'Quiet' before the alert barking habit becomes entrenched.
diet_notes: Strictly weight-managed diet. Even slightly overweight is dangerous for the spine. Lean protein, measured portions, minimal treats. Avoid any food that encourages weight gain.
exercise_notes: 30-60 minutes daily. Moderate walks, indoor play, scent games. Avoid stairs, jumping, and rough play. Swimming is excellent — low-impact but good exercise. Always support the back when picking up.
grooming_notes: Depends on coat type. Smooth: minimal, weekly wipe-down. Longhaired: daily brushing. Wirehaired: hand-stripping twice yearly plus regular brushing. All types: regular nail trims and ear cleaning.

name: German Shorthaired Pointer
common_training_challenges: Extreme energy requiring significant daily exercise
Can be destructive without sufficient outlets
training_tips: Exercise is prerequisite to training. A tired GSP is a trainable GSP.
Channel prey drive into retrieval and scent work. This gives their instinct a legitimate outlet.
Off-leash reliability is essential but challenging due to prey drive. Use a long line for months before trusting recall.
Vary training activities. GSPs are versatile and bore with repetition.
Start mental stimulation early. Puzzle toys and training games prevent the destructive boredom behaviours.
recommended_training_style: Active, varied, field-based when possible. Combine physical and mental challenges.

common_conditions.description: Joint malformation in this active breed.
Inherited eye condition affecting colour vision.
Swelling due to lymphatic system issues.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 12-18 months for joint development.
breed_description:
The German Shorthaired Pointer is a do-everything sporting dog — a tireless athlete that excels in the field, on the trail, and in the home. GSPs were bred to point, retrieve, and track game on both land and water, and that versatility translates to a dog that can do virtually anything.

This is not a breed for the sedentary. GSPs need serious exercise — 90+ minutes daily of vigorous activity. Without it, they'll channel their considerable energy into redecorating your home (and not in a good way). They're the marathon runners of the dog world.

In the right active home, a GSP is an incredible companion: affectionate, eager to please, and endlessly enthusiastic about any outdoor adventure. They're excellent family dogs who are gentle with children and friendly with everyone.
history: Developed in 19th-century Germany as the ultimate all-purpose hunting dog. Created by crossing various German pointing breeds with Bloodhounds and English Pointers. The goal was a versatile gun dog that could point, retrieve on land and water, and track wounded game. AKC recognised in 1930.
fun_facts: GSPs are one of the most versatile sporting breeds — they point, retrieve, and track.
They've competed in virtually every dog sport: agility, dock diving, flyball, and more.
A GSP named C.J. won Best in Show at Westminster in 2016.
They have webbed feet for powerful swimming.
puppy_tips: Exercise BEFORE training. A GSP with pent-up energy cannot focus.
Start recall training with a long line from day one. Prey drive will test every recall.
Provide mental enrichment: puzzle toys, nose work, and varied training exercises.
Socialise with smaller animals carefully. Strong prey drive means small pets may be at risk.
Begin field or scent work if possible. This channels their natural instincts productively.
diet_notes: High-energy breed requiring performance-level nutrition. High protein, moderate fat. Multiple meals daily to reduce bloat risk. Increase portions during heavy exercise periods.
exercise_notes: 90-150 minutes daily of vigorous exercise. Running, swimming, hiking, and field work are ideal. This breed needs to RUN, not just walk. Without adequate exercise, behaviour problems are virtually guaranteed.
grooming_notes: Low-maintenance coat. Weekly brushing. Occasional baths. Check ears after outdoor activities for debris. Regular nail trims.

name: Pembroke Welsh Corgi
common_training_challenges: Can be bossy with other dogs
Prone to weight gain affecting spine
training_tips: Address heel-nipping immediately — this is hardwired herding behaviour, not aggression.
Teach 'Quiet' early. Corgis bark at everything that moves.
Keep weight lean — extra weight on that long spine causes disc problems.
Channel herding instinct into structured activities: agility, herding trials, or obedience.
Socialise with other dogs carefully. Corgis can be bossy and provoke larger dogs.
recommended_training_style: Structured, consistent, redirect herding instincts into activities

common_conditions.description: Long spine is vulnerable to disc issues.
Despite small size, Corgis are affected.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The Pembroke Welsh Corgi packs the attitude of a large herding dog into a compact, low-rider package. Originally bred to herd cattle by nipping at their heels, Corgis are bold, intelligent, and surprisingly athletic for their size.

Don't let the cute factor fool you — Corgis are working dogs with strong opinions. They're quick learners who can also be quick to take charge if you let them. Their herding instinct means they'll try to manage children, other pets, and guests by nipping at ankles.

Famously favoured by Queen Elizabeth II, Corgis have surged in popularity. They're wonderful companions for active owners who appreciate a dog with personality. Weight management is critical — their long spine and short legs make obesity particularly dangerous.
history: Ancient Welsh herding breed dating back to the 10th century. Legend says fairies gifted them to Welsh farmers. The 'Pembroke' variety (no tail) originated in Pembrokeshire, Wales. Queen Elizabeth II owned more than 30 Corgis during her reign. AKC recognised in 1934.
fun_facts: Queen Elizabeth II owned over 30 Pembroke Welsh Corgis during her reign.
According to Welsh legend, Corgis were the steeds of woodland fairies.
Despite their small size, they can herd cattle 10x their weight by nipping at heels.
A group of Corgis is sometimes called a 'stumble'.
puppy_tips: Redirect heel-nipping to toys from day one. Provide a clear alternative behaviour.
Start weight management immediately. Measure food precisely — Corgis will overeat.
Socialise with larger dogs carefully. Corgis don't know they're small and can provoke bigger dogs.
Use ramps for furniture. Protect that long spine from jumping.
Begin grooming early — Corgis shed heavily and need to accept brushing as a routine.
diet_notes: Strictly portion-controlled. Corgis gain weight looking at food. Use kibble as training treats from daily allowance. Low-calorie treats only.
exercise_notes: 60-90 minutes daily. Active walks, agility, herding activities if available. Despite short legs, they have surprising stamina. Avoid excessive jumping — protect the spine.
grooming_notes: Heavy shedding year-round, extreme during coat blows. Daily brushing during shedding season, 2-3x weekly otherwise. Regular nail trims. Ear cleaning weekly.

name: Australian Shepherd
coat_type: Double coat, medium length, wavy to straight
common_training_challenges: Needs a job or becomes neurotic
Herding family members and other pets
training_tips: This breed NEEDS mental work. Training sessions, puzzle toys, and activities should be daily.
Channel herding instinct: agility, flyball, herding, disc dog — anything structured and active.
Socialise with strangers early. Aussies can be reserved or suspicious without positive exposure.
Train an 'off switch'. Aussies need to learn to settle as much as they need to learn to work.
Teach impulse control around movement. Running children, bikes, and cars trigger herding behaviour.
recommended_training_style: Task-oriented, variety-rich, combine physical and mental challenges. Give them a job.

common_conditions.description: Seizure disorder with moderate breed incidence.
Drug sensitivity affecting many herding breeds.
Eye clouding, can lead to blindness.

symptoms: Can affect vision in severe cases

medication_sensitivities: Certain anaesthetics — inform vet of MDR1 status
spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 12-18 months for joint development.
breed_description:
The Australian Shepherd is a brilliant, driven herding dog that thrives with a purpose. Despite the name, Aussies were developed in the American West as ranch dogs. Their intelligence, athleticism, and work ethic make them exceptional partners for active owners.

Aussies are not casual pets. They need significant daily mental and physical stimulation — without it, they become anxious, destructive, and neurotic. This breed is at their best when they have a job: agility, herding, search and rescue, or simply managing a complex training routine.

With the right owner, an Australian Shepherd is an extraordinary companion. They're loyal, responsive, and capable of learning virtually anything. Their striking merle coats and keen expression make them head-turners at every park.
history: Developed in the American West in the 19th century from European herding dogs that came via Australia (hence the misleading name). Perfected by Basque shepherds who emigrated from Australia to California. Became popular through rodeo performances in the 1950s-60s. AKC recognised in 1991.
fun_facts: Despite the name, Australian Shepherds were developed in America, not Australia.
They're one of the top breeds in agility competition worldwide.
Aussies often have heterochromia — two different-coloured eyes.
They were popularised by trick-performing rodeo dogs in the 1950s.
puppy_tips: Provide mental challenges from day one. Puzzle toys, training games, and novel experiences prevent boredom.
Begin socialisation with strangers early. Aussies can become suspicious of new people without positive exposure.
Teach a 'settle' command. Aussies need to learn to relax, not just work.
Channel herding instinct into structured activities. Without an outlet, they'll herd your children.
Check MDR1 status immediately. This affects which medications are safe for your dog.
diet_notes: High-quality, performance-level nutrition. Active dogs need higher protein and fat. Adjust portions to activity level — ranch Aussies need more than suburban Aussies.
exercise_notes: 90-150 minutes daily of vigorous exercise AND mental stimulation. Running, agility, herding, hiking, and disc games. A walk around the block is not enough for this breed.
grooming_notes: Moderate to heavy shedding. Brush 2-3x weekly, daily during shedding season. Never shave a double coat — it doesn't grow back properly. Regular ear cleaning and nail trims.

name: Yorkshire Terrier
common_training_challenges: Small dog syndrome — can become bossy if not trained
Can be snappy if not socialised
training_tips: Treat your Yorkie like a real dog, not a fashion accessory. Same rules as any breed.
Housetraining takes longer with toy breeds. Use indoor potty options as backup.
Socialise with other dogs and people to prevent small-dog-syndrome aggression.
Keep sessions short and fun. Yorkies are smart but don't tolerate boring repetition.
Teach bite inhibition even though bites are tiny. Bad habits at 5 lbs are still bad habits.
recommended_training_style: Positive, fun sessions. Treat like a real dog. Consistent rules despite small size.

common_conditions.description: Kneecap dislocation very common in toy breeds.
Abnormal blood vessel bypasses the liver.
Low blood sugar, especially in puppies.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-9 months) but discuss with vet given tiny size.
breed_description:
The Yorkshire Terrier is a tiny dog with the heart of a terrier — bold, confident, and utterly convinced they're the biggest dog in the room. Originally bred as ratters in Yorkshire's textile mills, Yorkies have traded the factory floor for the lap of luxury without losing an ounce of their spirited personality.

Yorkies are affectionate, loyal companions who bond deeply with their owners. Their hypoallergenic, silky coat makes them popular with allergy sufferers, though it requires significant grooming commitment.

The biggest training challenge with Yorkies is taking them seriously. Many owners inadvertently create 'small dog syndrome' by not applying the same training standards they'd use for larger breeds. A well-trained Yorkie is delightful; an untrained one is a tiny tyrant.
history: Developed in 19th-century Yorkshire, England, by Scottish weavers who brought their terriers south during the Industrial Revolution. Originally working ratters in textile mills, they caught the attention of Victorian ladies who refined them into companion dogs. AKC recognised in 1885.
fun_facts: Yorkies were originally working dogs bred to catch rats in textile mills.
A famous Yorkie named Smoky served in WWII, running telegraph wire through narrow pipes.
Despite their tiny size, they were one of the first therapy dogs.
Their coat is more similar to human hair than typical dog fur.
puppy_tips: Monitor for hypoglycaemia in young puppies. Keep honey or sugar water on hand for emergencies.
Use a harness, never a collar. Tracheal collapse risk makes collar pressure dangerous.
Begin dental care from puppyhood. Brush teeth daily — Yorkies are extremely prone to dental disease.
Socialise with larger dogs carefully. Teach them appropriate caution without creating fear.
Start grooming routines immediately. Their coat needs daily maintenance throughout life.
diet_notes: High-quality small-breed formula. Feed 3-4 small meals daily (especially puppies) to prevent hypoglycaemia. Dental kibble can help with tooth health.
exercise_notes: 30-60 minutes daily. Indoor play, short walks, and training sessions. Yorkies are surprisingly active but tire quickly. Avoid extreme temperatures.
grooming_notes: Daily brushing essential. Monthly professional grooming for full coat or opt for a puppy clip. Regular dental care — brush teeth daily. Tear stain cleaning. Nail trims every 2 weeks.

name: Cavalier King Charles Spaniel
coat_type: Single coat, medium length, silky with feathering
common_training_challenges: Can be too soft for harsh corrections
Easily distracted by birds and butterflies
training_tips: Cavaliers are extremely sensitive — only positive reinforcement works. Harsh words genuinely upset them.
Address separation anxiety from day one. Build independence gradually, not all at once.
Training sessions should be gentle and fun. This breed wilts under pressure.
Socialise confidently. Cavaliers are naturally friendly but can become timid if not properly exposed.
Use their food motivation. Cavaliers are foodies and learn quickly with treat rewards.
recommended_training_style: Gentle positive reinforcement, never harsh. Build confidence through success.

common_conditions.description: Heart condition affecting nearly all Cavaliers eventually.
Skull too small for brain, causing spinal fluid buildup.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The Cavalier King Charles Spaniel is the ultimate companion dog — gentle, affectionate, and happiest when they're on your lap. Bred specifically for companionship over centuries, Cavaliers have an almost magical ability to sense and match their owner's mood.

They're equally happy on a brisk walk or curled up on the sofa. This adaptability, combined with their sweet nature and gorgeous silky coat, makes them one of the most popular breeds for families, seniors, and first-time dog owners.

The breed's most significant challenge is health. Mitral valve disease and syringomyelia are prevalent and serious conditions. Choosing a reputable breeder who health-tests is critical, and regular cardiac screening throughout life is essential.
history: Named after King Charles II, who was so devoted to his spaniels that he allegedly neglected affairs of state for them. The modern Cavalier was re-created in the 1920s when a wealthy American offered a prize for breeders who could reproduce the flat-nosed spaniels from historical paintings. AKC recognised in 1995.
fun_facts: King Charles II was said to have neglected running the country in favour of playing with his spaniels.
They're one of the few toy breeds originally bred as sporting dogs (flushing game).
Cavaliers are known as 'comfort spaniels' and are frequently used as therapy dogs.
They were the inspiration for Lady in Disney's Lady and the Tramp.
celebrity_dogs: Lady (inspiration for Lady and the Tramp)
Charlotte York's Elizabeth (Sex and the City)
puppy_tips: Begin cardiac monitoring early. Annual heart checks are essential for this breed.
Build independence gradually. Cavaliers are prone to separation anxiety — crate training and alone-time practice are critical.
Socialise gently but thoroughly. They're naturally friendly but can become timid.
Start grooming routines early. The feathered coat needs regular maintenance.
Use positive reinforcement only. This breed is too sensitive for any harsh training methods.
diet_notes: High-quality diet appropriate for size. Watch weight — extra weight stresses heart. Omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for heart health. Moderate portion sizes.
exercise_notes: 30-60 minutes daily. Moderate walks, gentle play, and training. Adaptable to owner's activity level. Not an extreme exercise breed but enjoys walks and games.
grooming_notes: Brush 2-3x weekly to prevent tangles in feathering. Regular ear cleaning — long ears trap moisture. Monthly professional grooming optional but helpful. Regular dental care.

name: Doberman Pinscher
common_training_challenges: High energy needs for a large breed
training_tips: Socialise extensively and early. Dobermans need positive experiences with strangers and dogs.
This breed learns incredibly fast. Start formal training from 8 weeks.
Physical AND mental exercise daily. Dobermans need both to be balanced.
Build confidence through positive exposure. A nervous Doberman is a dangerous Doberman.
Use their intelligence: agility, obedience, nose work, trick training all channel their drive.
recommended_training_style: Structured positive reinforcement. Fast-paced, challenging. They need to think.

common_conditions.description: Enlarged heart reducing pumping efficiency. Very prevalent in the breed.
Blood clotting disorder common in Dobermans.
Joint issues in this large, active breed.
Spinal cord compression in the neck.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 12-18 months for joint development.
breed_description:
The Doberman Pinscher is elegance and power combined — a sleek, athletic working dog with one of the keenest minds in the canine world. Originally created as the ultimate personal protection dog, today's Doberman is more companion than guard dog, though their protective instincts remain.

Dobermans are intensely loyal, often bonding to one person above all others. They're sometimes called 'Velcro dogs' for their desire to be physically close to their owner at all times. This devotion, combined with their intelligence, makes them highly responsive training partners.

The breed's biggest health concern is dilated cardiomyopathy — a heart condition that affects a significant percentage of Dobermans. Annual cardiac screening with a Holter monitor is essential. With proper health management, a Doberman is a stunning, devoted companion.
history: Created by Louis Dobermann, a German tax collector in the 1890s, who wanted a loyal protection dog to accompany him on his rounds. Likely bred from Rottweilers, German Pinschers, Greyhounds, and Weimaraners. The result was the ultimate guardian breed. AKC recognised in 1908.
fun_facts: Louis Dobermann created the breed because he needed protection while collecting taxes.
Dobermans served as official war dogs of the US Marine Corps in WWII.
They're one of the top 5 most intelligent dog breeds.
A Doberman named Kurt was the first canine casualty in the Battle of Guam and has a memorial.
puppy_tips: Start socialisation immediately and continue throughout life. This is non-negotiable for the breed.
Begin formal obedience training at 8 weeks. Dobermans learn fast and good habits need to form first.
Provide daily physical and mental exercise. A bored Doberman with energy is destructive.
Schedule cardiac screening early and maintain annual checks. DCM is life-threatening and often silent.
Handle extensively: paws, mouth, ears, body. Vets need to be able to examine this large, powerful dog.
diet_notes: High-quality, high-protein diet. Avoid grain-free diets (linked to heart issues in some breeds). Taurine supplementation may benefit heart health — discuss with vet.
exercise_notes: 60-120 minutes daily. Running, hiking, agility, and obedience training are ideal. Dobermans need vigorous exercise, not just walks. Mental exercise equally important.
grooming_notes: Very low maintenance. Weekly wipe-down with a damp cloth. Occasional brush. Regular nail trims. Minimal shedding.

name: Boxer
common_training_challenges: Mouthy play (uses paws like a boxer)
training_tips: Boxers are eternal puppies — expect puppy energy until age 3-4.
Jumping is the #1 issue. Start 'four on the floor' from day one.
Short, high-energy training sessions. Boxers lose interest fast but give 100% while engaged.
Avoid heat. Brachycephalic structure makes overheating dangerous.
Use play as reward alongside treats. Boxers are play-motivated.
recommended_training_style: Energetic, play-based, short sessions. Use their goofiness, don't fight it.

common_conditions.description: Boxers have one of the highest cancer rates of any breed.
Joint issues in active large breed.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 12-18 months for joint development.
breed_description:
The Boxer is the clown prince of the dog world — a muscular, athletic dog with an irrepressible sense of fun. They're called Boxers because they literally box with their front paws during play, standing on hind legs and sparring like their namesake.

Boxers are wonderful family dogs with an almost supernatural gentleness around children. They're protective without being aggressive, playful without being rough (with kids), and loyal to their entire family unit.

The breed matures slowly — very slowly. Expect a large, powerful dog with full puppy brain until age 3-4. Patience during the adolescent years is essential. Health-wise, cancer is the breed's biggest concern, with cardiac issues close behind.
history: Developed in 19th-century Germany from the now-extinct Bullenbeisser (bull-biter) breed, crossed with English Bulldogs. Originally used for bull-baiting and hunting large game. Transitioned to companion and working dog roles. AKC recognised in 1904.
fun_facts: Boxers literally 'box' — they use their front paws in play, standing on hind legs.
They don't fully mature mentally until 3-4 years old, one of the longest puppyhoods.
Boxers served as messenger dogs in both World Wars.
The breed holds the Guinness record for the longest tongue on a dog (17 inches).
puppy_tips: Start jump training immediately. At 70 lbs, an untrained jumping Boxer is a hazard.
Exercise before every training session. A Boxer with energy cannot focus.
Channel mouthy play into appropriate toys. They use their paws and mouths in play naturally.
Train for heat awareness. Know the signs of overheating and always have water available.
Socialise with other dogs extensively. Boxers are generally friendly but can be overwhelming in their enthusiasm.
diet_notes: High-quality protein diet. Feed 2-3 meals daily to reduce bloat risk. Avoid overfeeding — monitor weight. Some Boxers have sensitive stomachs requiring limited-ingredient diets.
exercise_notes: 60-120 minutes daily of vigorous exercise. Running, play, agility, and active games. Mental stimulation important. Avoid exercise in heat — brachycephalic structure limits cooling.
grooming_notes: Minimal grooming. Weekly brushing. Occasional baths. Regular nail trims. Clean facial wrinkles if present. Wipe coat with damp cloth between baths.

name: Miniature Schnauzer
coat_type: Double coat, wiry outer coat, soft undercoat
common_training_challenges: Can be scrappy with other dogs
training_tips: Address barking early. Teach 'Quiet' as a core command — Schnauzers alert bark at everything.
Channel terrier energy into structured activities and trick training.
Socialise with other dogs carefully. Mini Schnauzers can be feisty with dogs they don't know.
Vary training to prevent boredom. They're smart but lose interest with repetition.
Grooming is part of training — start handling and grooming routines from puppyhood.
recommended_training_style: Engaging, varied, firm but fair. Channel terrier attitude productively.

common_conditions.description: Inflammation of the pancreas, often diet-related.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The Miniature Schnauzer is a terrier with a touch of sophistication — smart, alert, and packed with personality. Their distinctive bearded face and bushy eyebrows give them a wise, almost professorial look that matches their clever temperament.

Mini Schnauzers are excellent family dogs: they're great with children, adaptable to apartment or house living, and hypoallergenic. They're also natural watchdogs who will alert you to every delivery, passing squirrel, and suspicious leaf.

The barking is the breed's most common training challenge. They're alert to everything and vocal about it. Early 'Quiet' training is essential. Health-wise, pancreatitis and high cholesterol are the main concerns — a low-fat diet is important.
history: Developed in Germany in the late 1800s by crossing the Standard Schnauzer with smaller breeds including Poodles and Affenpinschers. Originally ratters and farm dogs. The distinctive beard and eyebrows served as protection from vermin bites. AKC recognised in 1926.
fun_facts: The beard originally protected their face from rats and other vermin they hunted.
They're the most popular of the three Schnauzer sizes (Miniature, Standard, Giant).
Mini Schnauzers are one of the few terrier breeds that are typically good with children.
Their name comes from the German word 'Schnauze' meaning snout or muzzle.
puppy_tips: Start bark management from day one. Teach 'Quiet' before it becomes habitual.
Begin grooming routines immediately. Professional grooming is a lifelong commitment.
Socialise with other dogs in controlled settings. Mini Schnauzers can be scrappy.
Provide varied mental stimulation. They're too smart for repetitive training.
Watch fat intake from puppyhood. This breed is genetically prone to pancreatitis.
diet_notes: Low-fat diet is critical due to pancreatitis risk. Avoid high-fat treats and table scraps. High-quality protein with moderate fat content. Monitor weight.
exercise_notes: 45-75 minutes daily. Active walks, play sessions, and training. Good stamina for their size. Enjoy agility and earth dog trials.
grooming_notes: Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks for hand-stripping or clipping. Brush 2-3x weekly between sessions. Daily beard cleaning after meals. Regular ear cleaning.

name: Cane Corso
common_training_challenges: Can be dog-aggressive without extensive socialisation
Enormous strength requires early leash training
Can be aloof or suspicious with strangers
training_tips: Socialisation is THE most critical investment. Start immediately, continue relentlessly.
Establish yourself as a calm, confident leader. Corsos need clear, fair boundaries.
Leash manners must be taught while they're small. A 110 lb dog that pulls is unmanageable.
Expose to strangers positively and frequently. Corsos are natural guardians — they don't need encouragement to protect.
Professional training classes are strongly recommended. This breed benefits from structured group settings.
recommended_training_style: Calm, confident leadership. Structured, consistent. Early professional training recommended.

common_conditions.description: Deep-chested giant breed at high risk.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Wait until 18-24 months for giant breed joint development.
breed_description:
The Cane Corso is an Italian mastiff of impressive stature and noble bearing. Originally bred as a guardian of property and family, the Corso combines power, intelligence, and a calm confidence that makes them one of the most imposing yet tractable of the giant breeds.

In the right home — experienced owners with space, time for training, and understanding of guardian breeds — a Cane Corso is a magnificent companion. They're deeply loyal, surprisingly gentle with their family, and naturally protective without needing aggression training.

This is an advanced breed, not suited to first-time owners. Extensive socialisation, professional training, and experienced handling are essential. Without proper guidance, their guardian instinct and massive size can become liabilities.
history: Ancient Italian breed descended from Roman war dogs (Canis Pugnax). Used to guard property, hunt large game, and protect livestock. Nearly extinct by the 1970s but revived by Italian breed enthusiasts. The name comes from Latin 'cohors' meaning guardian or protector. AKC recognised in 2010.
fun_facts: Cane Corsos are descended from ancient Roman war dogs.
Their name means 'guardian dog' in Latin.
They were nearly extinct in the 1970s before Italian breeders revived the breed.
A Corso's bite force is approximately 700 PSI — one of the strongest of any domestic dog.
puppy_tips: Begin socialisation the day you bring them home. Every day without socialisation is a missed opportunity.
Enrol in professional puppy classes immediately. Group training is essential for guardian breeds.
Teach leash manners while they're light enough to manage. This becomes impossible at 100+ lbs.
Handle extensively: every part of their body, every day. Vets and groomers need to work with them safely.
Establish calm, consistent house rules. A Corso needs to know the boundaries from the start.
diet_notes: Giant breed puppy formula during growth, then large breed adult. Feed 2-3 meals daily to reduce bloat risk. Don't overfeed during growth — excess weight damages developing joints.
exercise_notes: 60-90 minutes daily. Moderate to active exercise. Walks, training, and mental stimulation. Avoid intense exercise during growth period. Not an endurance breed — they prefer purposeful activity.
grooming_notes: Low maintenance coat. Weekly brushing. Drool management if your Corso is a drooler. Regular nail trims, ear cleaning.

name: Great Dane
common_training_challenges: Giant size makes any behaviour problem a BIG problem
Counter surfing is effortless at their height
training_tips: Everything must be trained while they're small. At 150 lbs, bad habits are unmanageable.
Leash manners are critical. Start at 8 weeks when they're still light enough to handle.
Socialise gently. Great Danes can be timid — build confidence through positive exposure.
Keep training sessions short and sweet. Danes lose focus quickly but respond to gentle patience.
Teach 'Off' and 'Place' early. A Great Dane that jumps or counter surfs is a safety hazard.
recommended_training_style: Gentle, patient, short sessions. Train while they're small. Confidence-building focus.

common_conditions.description: The #1 killer of Great Danes. Emergency condition.
Joint malformation from carrying immense weight.
Bone cancer prevalent in giant breeds.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Wait until 18-24 months minimum for giant breed development.
breed_description:
The Great Dane is the gentle giant of the dog world — a towering, majestic breed with a heart as big as their frame. Despite their imposing size, Danes are famously gentle, patient, and affectionate, often convinced they're lap dogs.

Great Danes are wonderful family companions who are gentle with children and friendly with everyone. Their calm demeanour makes them surprisingly good apartment dogs (if you have the space), as they're less active than many smaller breeds.

The trade-off for this magnificent breed is a shorter lifespan and significant health concerns. Bloat (GDV) is the most urgent — every Great Dane owner should know the signs and have an emergency vet plan. Their hearts, joints, and bones all carry the strain of supporting a giant body.
history: Despite the name, Great Danes were developed in Germany, not Denmark. They descend from ancient war dogs and were refined as boar hunters. German nobility prized them as chamber dogs (Kammerhunde) — guard dogs that slept in their masters' bedrooms. AKC recognised in 1887.
fun_facts: Great Danes are German, not Danish — the name is a historical misnomer.
Scooby-Doo is a Great Dane, though a notably un-brave one.
They were originally bred to hunt wild boar, one of the most dangerous prey animals.
A Great Dane named Zeus holds the record for tallest dog ever at 44 inches at the shoulder.
puppy_tips: Learn bloat (GDV) signs immediately. This is a life-threatening emergency and minutes matter.
Feed elevated bowls and 2-3 small meals daily. Avoid exercise 1 hour before and after meals. These reduce bloat risk.
Train everything while they're small. An untrained 150 lb dog is a genuine safety hazard.
Socialise gently. Danes can be timid — force-free exposure builds confidence.
Invest in giant-breed appropriate supplies: beds, crates, harnesses. Standard sizes won't work.
diet_notes: Giant breed puppy formula with controlled calcium and phosphorus during growth. Adult: high-quality large breed formula. Feed 2-3 meals daily (never one large meal) to reduce bloat risk. Elevated bowls. Rest after eating.
exercise_notes: 30-60 minutes daily. Moderate walks and gentle play. NOT a jogging partner. Avoid intense exercise during growth (first 24 months). Mental stimulation through training. Short play sessions.
grooming_notes: Easy coat care. Weekly brushing. Occasional baths. Regular nail trims (giant nails!). Drool management — keep towels handy.

name: Shih Tzu
grooming_frequency: Daily brushing or puppy clip monthly
common_training_challenges: Grooming resistance if not started early
training_tips: Housetraining requires patience — small bladders and a regal attitude mean accidents happen longer.
Keep sessions short and fun. Shih Tzus are companions, not working dogs. 3-5 minutes max.
Use high-value, smelly treats. Standard kibble won't motivate this breed.
Start grooming routines from day one. Daily brushing must become an accepted part of life.
Socialise with bigger dogs to build appropriate confidence and respect for size differences.
recommended_training_style: Short, treat-motivated, gentle, patient. Make it their idea.

common_conditions.description: Prominent eyes are vulnerable to injury and disease.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The Shih Tzu is royalty in a small package — bred for centuries as companions for Chinese emperors, they maintain that regal bearing today. With their flowing coat, pushed-in face, and confident stride, Shih Tzus carry themselves with an air of dignified importance.

Beneath the aristocratic exterior is a warm, playful, and surprisingly sturdy little dog. Shih Tzus are adaptable companions equally happy in an apartment or a mansion. They're great with children, other pets, and visitors — few breeds are as universally friendly.

Grooming is the biggest commitment. Their beautiful coat requires daily maintenance or a practical puppy clip. The brachycephalic face needs regular cleaning and comes with breathing considerations, though generally less severe than Bulldogs or Frenchies.
history: One of the oldest dog breeds, dating back to ancient China. Bred in the Chinese imperial palace as companion dogs for royalty. The name means 'lion dog' — they were bred to resemble lions, which hold significance in Buddhist mythology. Nearly went extinct after the Chinese Revolution. AKC recognised in 1969.
fun_facts: Shih Tzus were bred as palace dogs for Chinese emperors and were not available to commoners.
The name means 'lion dog' in Mandarin.
They're one of the 14 oldest dog breeds, closely related to wolves genetically.
All modern Shih Tzus descend from just 14 dogs that survived the Chinese Communist Revolution.
puppy_tips: Start grooming from day one. Daily brushing is non-negotiable for this coat.
Clean face and eyes daily. Prominent eyes are prone to irritation and the wrinkles trap moisture.
Begin dental care immediately. Brush teeth daily — Shih Tzus are extremely prone to dental disease.
Housetraining: use a consistent schedule and be patient. Consider indoor potty options as backup.
Socialise gently but thoroughly. Shih Tzus are naturally friendly but need positive experiences to stay that way.
diet_notes: Small breed formula. Watch weight — small frame means extra weight shows fast. Dental kibble can help. Feed 2-3 small meals daily.
exercise_notes: 20-40 minutes daily. Short walks and indoor play. Not an athletic breed. Avoid heat and humidity due to brachycephalic breathing. Mental stimulation through training games.
grooming_notes: Daily brushing essential for full coat. Most pet owners opt for a short puppy clip maintained every 4-6 weeks. Daily face cleaning around eyes and nose. Regular ear cleaning. Daily dental care.

name: Siberian Husky
coat_type: Double coat, medium length, very thick
grooming_frequency: 2-3x per week, daily during coat blows
common_training_challenges: Escape artists — will jump, dig, and squeeze through anything
training_tips: NEVER trust a Husky off-lead in an unfenced area. Their recall is unreliable by design — they were bred to run.
Secure your yard completely: 6-foot fences with dig guards and no gaps. Huskies are legendary escape artists.
Manage expectations. Huskies are independent thinkers, not eager-to-please dogs.
Exercise, exercise, exercise. A tired Husky is a manageable Husky. 90+ minutes daily.
Provide mental stimulation and outlets for their intelligence. Puzzle toys, nose work, and training variety help.
recommended_training_style: Patient, consistent, manage expectations. Don't expect Lab-like obedience. Make training fun.

common_conditions.description: Joint issues in this active breed.
Can cause skin and coat issues.
symptoms: Hair loss around eyes and mouth

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (12-18 months recommended).
breed_description:
The Siberian Husky is freedom incarnate — a stunningly beautiful, independent-minded sled dog that will challenge everything you think you know about dog training. Bred to run vast distances in the harshest conditions on Earth, Huskies bring that same tireless energy and stubborn determination to suburban life.

Huskies are not for everyone. They shed constantly, howl rather than bark, escape from any enclosure they find boring, and consider recall commands to be polite suggestions. But for active owners who appreciate their spirited independence, Huskies are endlessly entertaining companions.

The Husky's striking appearance — blue eyes, wolf-like features, dramatic markings — makes them one of the most photographed breeds. Just be prepared: that beauty comes with a LOT of fur.
history: Developed by the Chukchi people of northeastern Siberia as endurance sled dogs, Huskies could pull light loads over vast distances in extreme cold. Brought to Alaska during the Nome Gold Rush. The legendary 1925 serum run (inspiration for the Iditarod) featured Siberian Huskies. AKC recognised in 1930.
fun_facts: Balto, a Siberian Husky, led the final leg of the 1925 serum run that saved Nome, Alaska.
Huskies can run 100+ miles per day and maintain a pace for hours.
They have a unique metabolism that allows them to run long distances without fatiguing.
Huskies can have bi-coloured eyes (heterochromia) — one blue and one brown.
celebrity_dogs: Togo (the true hero of the serum run)
puppy_tips: Secure your property immediately. Huskies can jump 6-foot fences, dig under walls, and open gates.
Accept that off-leash reliability may never happen. Use long lines for exercise in open areas.
Provide massive amounts of exercise. Running, bikejoring, skijoring, or long hikes daily.
Get a good vacuum. Husky shedding is legendary — they 'blow' their coat twice yearly.
Supervise around small pets. Huskies have strong prey drive towards cats and small animals.
diet_notes: Surprisingly efficient eaters for their size. Moderate portions of high-quality food. They naturally regulate intake better than most breeds. Increase during heavy exercise periods.
exercise_notes: 90-150 minutes daily MINIMUM. Running, mushing, hiking, or active play. Simple walks are not enough. They need to RUN. Avoid exercise in heat — they're designed for cold weather.
grooming_notes: Extreme shedding. Brush 2-3x weekly, daily during coat blows (2x yearly). Never shave a double coat — it insulates against both heat and cold. Regular nail trims.

name: Bernese Mountain Dog
common_training_challenges: Slow to mature physically and mentally
Can be sensitive to harsh corrections
Short lifespan causes emotional preparation challenges
training_tips: Berners are gentle souls — always use positive, patient training methods.
Start leash training while they're small. An 80 lb Berner that pulls is unmanageable.
Socialise gently and broadly. They're naturally friendly but can be shy.
Train basic obedience by 4 months. They grow fast and habits set early.
Prepare emotionally for a shorter lifespan. This is the breed's most difficult reality.
recommended_training_style: Gentle, positive, patient. Never harsh. Build confidence through success.

common_conditions.description: Berners have one of the highest cancer rates of any breed.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Wait until 18-24 months for giant breed development.
breed_description:
The Bernese Mountain Dog is a gentle giant with a heart of gold. Originally Swiss farm dogs bred for drafting (pulling carts), herding, and guarding, Berners combine impressive size with a remarkably sweet, calm temperament.

Berners are exceptional family dogs — patient with children, friendly with everyone, and content to be wherever their people are. They're one of the few giant breeds that are genuinely laid-back indoors while still capable of serious work outdoors.

The breed's greatest challenge is their health, particularly their cancer risk and relatively short lifespan (7-10 years). This reality makes every day with a Bernese Mountain Dog precious. Responsible breeders are working to improve longevity through health testing.
history: Developed in the Swiss Alps (Bern region) as versatile farm dogs. Used to pull carts of milk and cheese to market, guard property, and herd cattle. One of four Swiss Mountain Dog breeds. Nearly went extinct in the early 1900s before breed enthusiasts revived them. AKC recognised in 1937.
fun_facts: Berners were used to pull carts of milk and cheese through the Swiss Alps.
They're one of few breeds that were specifically bred for cart-pulling (drafting).
The breed nearly went extinct in the early 1900s and was saved by Swiss enthusiasts.
Their tricolor coat (black, white, rust) is a breed hallmark — no other colour patterns occur.
puppy_tips: Enjoy every day. Berners have shorter lifespans than most breeds — make the most of it.
Start training while they're small. At 100 lbs, bad habits are permanent.
Socialise gently. Berners are naturally friendly but can be sensitive to overwhelming experiences.
Manage heat exposure. Their thick coat makes them heat-sensitive. Cool environments are essential.
Begin grooming routines early. Their long coat needs significant regular maintenance.
diet_notes: Giant breed puppy formula during growth. Adult: large breed formula. Feed 2-3 meals daily to reduce bloat risk. Monitor weight — excess weight stresses joints and heart.
exercise_notes: 30-60 minutes daily. Moderate walks, gentle hikes, and swimming. Not an endurance breed. Avoid intense exercise during growth and in hot weather. Cart-pulling is a wonderful breed-specific activity.
grooming_notes: Heavy shedding. Brush 2-3x weekly, daily during seasonal coat blows. Regular ear cleaning. Professional grooming optional. Drool management for some individuals.

name: Pomeranian
coat_type: Double coat, long, fluffy, thick with distinctive ruff
common_training_challenges: Barking at everything — they think they're guard dogs
training_tips: Treat your Pomeranian as a real dog. Same rules, boundaries, and training as any breed.
Barking management is critical. Teach 'Quiet' early and consistently.
Housetraining requires patience. Small bladder + tiny body = frequent accidents. Indoor potty as backup.
Socialise with larger dogs safely. Poms don't know they're small and can provoke bigger dogs.
Grooming must start early. Their fluffy coat needs regular maintenance and they must accept handling.
recommended_training_style: Gentle but firm consistency. Same standards as a big dog. Short, fun sessions.

common_conditions.description: Kneecap dislocation very common in toy breeds.
Hair loss condition specific to Pomeranians.
Low blood sugar, especially in small individuals.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-9 months).
breed_description:
The Pomeranian is a tiny fox-like dog with the confidence of a lion. Descended from large Arctic sled dogs, Pomeranians were bred down to toy size but retained every ounce of their ancestor's boldness and spirit.

Poms are vivacious, curious, and surprisingly intelligent. They make excellent companions for apartment dwellers and are particularly popular with owners who appreciate a dog with personality. Their fluffy coat and foxy face make them impossibly cute.

The biggest challenges are barking (they alert to everything), housetraining (small bladder, big opinions), and the temptation to spoil them. A Pomeranian treated as a fashion accessory rather than a dog develops behaviour problems quickly.
history: Descended from large Spitz-type sled dogs of Iceland and Lapland. Named after the Pomerania region (modern Poland/Germany). Queen Victoria famously bred smaller Pomeranians, reducing the breed's size by 50% during her reign. AKC recognised in 1888.
fun_facts: Pomeranians descended from large sled dogs — they were originally 30+ lbs.
Queen Victoria's love of small Poms reduced the breed's average size by half.
Two Pomeranians survived the Titanic — both from first-class cabins.
They're the most popular toy breed in many countries worldwide.
celebrity_dogs: Boo (the 'World's Cutest Dog' social media star)
puppy_tips: Use a harness, never a collar. Tracheal collapse risk makes collar pressure dangerous.
Start dental care immediately. Brush teeth daily — tiny mouths are prone to severe dental disease.
Monitor for hypoglycaemia, especially in very small individuals. Keep honey on hand.
Begin grooming early. Daily brushing prevents the coat from matting.
Socialise carefully with larger dogs. Supervise all interactions due to size vulnerability.
diet_notes: Small breed formula. Feed 3-4 small meals daily (especially young puppies) to prevent hypoglycaemia. Dental kibble can help. Watch treats — even small treats are calorie-significant for a 5 lb dog.
exercise_notes: 20-40 minutes daily. Short walks, indoor play, and training sessions. Not a high-endurance breed. Avoid extreme temperatures. Mental stimulation through trick training and puzzle toys.
grooming_notes: Brush 2-3x weekly minimum, daily if coat is long. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Never shave the double coat. Regular dental care. Tear stain cleaning.

name: Boston Terrier
training_tips: Keep sessions short and fun. Bostons are eager to please but overheat quickly.
Avoid training in heat. Air-conditioned environments are safest.
Bostons are naturally well-mannered — reinforce good behaviour generously.
