Protect those prominent eyes. Avoid rough play near the face.
Socialise broadly. Bostons are typically friendly but benefit from positive exposure.
recommended_training_style: Short, fun, positive sessions. Bostons are natural comedians — enjoy the training process.

common_conditions.description: Prominent eyes vulnerable to ulcers, cherry eye, cataracts.
Congenital deafness, especially in heavily white-marked dogs.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The Boston Terrier — the 'American Gentleman' — is a dapper, compact companion with impeccable manners and a wonderful sense of humour. One of the few truly American breeds, Bostons are friendly, adaptable, and endlessly entertaining.

Bostons are among the easiest breeds to train for first-time owners. They're naturally well-mannered, eager to please, and smart enough to learn quickly. Their tuxedo markings and expressive face give them a distinguished look that matches their gentle temperament.

Like all brachycephalic breeds, Bostons require heat management and monitoring for breathing issues. Their prominent eyes are also vulnerable to injury. Despite these health considerations, their wonderful personality makes them consistently popular companions.
history: One of the few breeds developed in America. Created in Boston in the late 1800s by crossing English Bulldogs with White English Terriers. Originally called 'Round Heads' or 'American Bull Terriers.' Named the official state dog of Massachusetts. AKC recognised in 1893.
fun_facts: Boston Terriers are one of the few breeds developed entirely in America.
They're the official state dog of Massachusetts.
Their tuxedo-like markings earned them the nickname 'American Gentleman.'
Bostons were one of the first breeds developed in the US for companionship, not work.
puppy_tips: Protect those prominent eyes from the start. Avoid sharp toys and rough facial play.
Heat management is essential. Bostons overheat quickly — exercise in cool conditions only.
Socialise broadly. Bostons are naturally friendly but benefit from varied positive experiences.
Start dental care early. Small brachycephalic jaws are prone to crowding.
Enjoy the training process — Bostons are natural comedians and training should be fun.
diet_notes: High-quality small breed formula. May need a limited-ingredient diet if allergies develop. Feed 2-3 small meals. Expect gas — it's a breed feature.
exercise_notes: 30-60 minutes daily. Short walks, indoor play, and training. Avoid heat and humidity. Not a distance running breed. Mental stimulation through games and training.
grooming_notes: Very low maintenance. Weekly brushing. Occasional bath. Clean facial wrinkles. Regular eye cleaning and monitoring for injuries.

name: Havanese
grooming_frequency: Daily brushing or corded, professional grooming monthly
training_tips: Address separation anxiety from day one. Build independence gradually with crate training.
Housetraining takes patience. Use consistent schedules and positive reinforcement.
Havanese are eager to please and learn tricks quickly. Use this natural aptitude.
Socialise broadly. They're naturally friendly but benefit from varied experiences.
Grooming must become routine from puppyhood. Daily brushing is a lifelong commitment.
recommended_training_style: Positive, gentle, fun. Trick training is their forte. Build independence alongside obedience.
spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The Havanese is Cuba's national dog — a small, sturdy, cheerful companion that was bred to charm. With their silky coat, expressive eyes, and springy gait, Havanese are natural entertainers who live to make their people laugh.

Havanese are exceptional trick dogs, often performing in circuses historically. They learn quickly, love performing, and have a natural showmanship that makes training genuinely fun. Their gentle, adaptable nature makes them ideal for families, apartments, and first-time owners.

The breed's main challenge is separation anxiety. Havanese bond intensely and don't cope well with being alone. Building independence from puppyhood and crate training are essential. Their coat is hypoallergenic but requires daily maintenance.
history: Cuba's only native breed, developed from extinct Bichon-type dogs brought by Spanish colonists. Bred as companions for Cuban aristocracy. When Cubans fled to America during the revolution, they brought their Havanese. The breed was re-established in the US from just 11 dogs. AKC recognised in 1996.
fun_facts: Havanese are the only dog breed native to Cuba.
They were historically known as circus dogs due to their natural performing ability.
All modern American Havanese descend from just 11 dogs brought during the Cuban Revolution.
Their springy gait (they appear to bounce) is a breed hallmark.
puppy_tips: Start independence training immediately. Crate training and alone-time practice prevent separation anxiety.
Begin daily grooming from puppyhood. The coat needs brushing every day.
Leverage their trick aptitude. Havanese are natural performers — trick training builds confidence and bond.
Socialise with dogs of all sizes. Their friendly nature usually makes this easy.
Housetraining: be patient and consistent. Use a regular schedule with lots of positive reinforcement.
diet_notes: Small breed formula. Watch weight. Feed 2-3 meals daily. Nothing special required — Havanese are generally easy keepers.
exercise_notes: 30-45 minutes daily. Moderate walks, indoor play, and training. Enjoy activities but don't need extreme exercise. Mental stimulation through trick training is ideal.
grooming_notes: Daily brushing essential for full coat. Many owners opt for a puppy clip for easier maintenance. Professional grooming monthly. Regular ear cleaning. Dental care.

name: English Springer Spaniel
coat_type: Double coat, medium length with feathering
training_tips: Channel their enthusiasm into structured activities. Springers LOVE having a job.
Teach impulse control early. Their excitement is endearing but needs management.
Use retrieval and scent work as training tools. They're bred for it and it's self-rewarding.
Socialise extensively. Springers are naturally friendly and socialisation reinforces this.
Provide adequate exercise. Underexercised Springers become anxious or destructive.
recommended_training_style: Enthusiastic, game-based, retrieval and scent work focus. Channel energy, don't suppress it.
spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (12-18 months recommended for joint health).
breed_description:
The English Springer Spaniel is the original 'gun dog' — a versatile, enthusiastic sporting breed that 'springs' game birds into the air for hunters. Today, they channel that same boundless energy and trainability into being exceptional family companions.

Springers are cheerful, affectionate dogs that seem to have a permanent smile. They adore their families, get along with everyone, and bring infectious enthusiasm to everything from training to walks to simply greeting you at the door.

They need significant exercise and mental stimulation. A Springer without enough activity becomes anxious or destructive. With adequate exercise and training, they're among the most rewarding breeds to own.
history: One of the oldest sporting breeds, dating back to the 1600s. 'Springing' spaniels flushed game birds into the air. The Springer and Cocker Spaniel were once the same breed — larger dogs were 'Springers' and smaller ones were 'Cockers.' Formally separated in the 1800s. AKC recognised in 1910.
fun_facts: Springers and Cocker Spaniels were once the same breed, sorted by size within litters.
The name 'Springer' comes from their job of 'springing' (flushing) game birds.
They have one of the best noses of any breed and are widely used as detection dogs.
English Springer Spaniels hold multiple records in field trial competitions.
puppy_tips: Provide at least 60 minutes of active exercise daily from the start.
Clean ears weekly. Long, floppy ears are infection magnets.
Start retrieval games early. It's their natural drive and the best training tool you have.
Socialise with other dogs and people extensively. Springers are naturally social.
Manage excitement around visitors. Springers greet everyone like a long-lost friend.
diet_notes: Active breed needing performance nutrition. High protein, moderate fat. Feed 2 meals daily. Increase portions during high-activity periods.
exercise_notes: 60-120 minutes daily of vigorous exercise. Running, swimming, retrieval, and hiking are ideal. Field work if available. Mental stimulation through scent games equally important.
grooming_notes: Brush 2x weekly. More during shedding. Feathered ears, chest, and legs need extra attention. Clean ears weekly. Professional trim every 8-12 weeks. Regular dental care.

name: Shetland Sheepdog
coat_type: Double coat, long, rough, with thick undercoat and mane
training_tips: Address barking early and consistently. Shelties bark at everything and it escalates.
Socialise with strangers extensively. Shelties can be reserved or shy without positive exposure.
Redirect herding instinct. They'll try to herd children and other pets by nipping.
Use positive methods only. Shelties are extremely sensitive and shut down with harsh corrections.
Shelties excel at agility and obedience. Channel their intelligence into structured activities.
recommended_training_style: Positive, gentle, challenge their intelligence. They live for pleasing their owner.
medication_sensitivities: Certain anaesthetics — inform vet of MDR1 status
spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The Shetland Sheepdog — or Sheltie — is a miniature version of the Rough Collie with all the intelligence and none of the size. Originally bred to herd sheep on Scotland's remote Shetland Islands, Shelties are one of the most trainable and devoted breeds in existence.

Shelties are brilliant students who consistently top obedience and agility rankings. They bond deeply with their families and are often described as 'shadow dogs' who follow their person from room to room. Their sensitivity makes them remarkably attuned to their owner's emotions.

The main challenges are barking (they're very vocal) and potential shyness with strangers. Early socialisation is essential. Their gorgeous coat requires significant grooming but their temperament makes every bit of maintenance worthwhile.
history: Developed on Scotland's Shetland Islands from Border Collies and other herding breeds, then crossed with smaller dogs. The harsh, isolated environment produced a small, hardy, intelligent herding dog. Originally called 'Shetland Collies' until Collie breeders objected. AKC recognised in 1911.
fun_facts: Shelties consistently rank in the top 10 most intelligent dog breeds.
They were originally called 'Shetland Collies' but Collie breeders protested the name.
Shelties excel in agility competition, often dominating the small dog classes.
On the Shetland Islands, everything is smaller — Shetland ponies, Shetland sheepdogs, even Shetland sheep.
puppy_tips: Begin socialisation with strangers immediately. Shelties can become fearful if not exposed early.
Teach 'Quiet' from the first week. Barking is the #1 behaviour issue in the breed.
Start grooming routines immediately. Daily brushing becomes essential as the adult coat grows.
Leverage their intelligence with trick training and agility. Shelties LOVE having a job.
Be gentle in all training. A harsh word can set a Sheltie back days. They take corrections very personally.
diet_notes: Standard small breed diet. Nothing special required. Watch weight — a sedentary Sheltie can gain weight. Feed measured portions.
exercise_notes: 60-90 minutes daily. Active walks, agility, obedience training, and games. Mental exercise is as important as physical. Shelties thrive in dog sports.
grooming_notes: Significant grooming commitment. Brush 2-3x weekly, daily during seasonal coat blows. Never shave the double coat. Mat-prone behind ears and on legs. Professional grooming optional but helpful.

name: Brittany
coat_type: Single coat, medium length, flat or wavy with feathering
common_training_challenges: Can be hyperactive without sufficient outlets
training_tips: Exercise is non-negotiable. 90+ minutes of vigorous activity daily.
Use gentle, positive methods only. Brittanys are extremely sensitive.
Channel their pointing and retrieving instincts into games and training.
Socialise early and often. Brittanys are naturally friendly and socialisation cements this.
Provide mental stimulation alongside physical exercise. Puzzle toys, nose work, field work.
recommended_training_style: Gentle positive reinforcement. Sensitive breed — never harsh. High-energy, game-based training.
spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (12-18 months recommended).
breed_description:
The Brittany is pure sporting dog energy in a medium-sized package. Originally bred as a versatile gun dog in France, Brittanys are tireless athletes with a gentle, eager-to-please temperament. They point, retrieve, and flush with equal enthusiasm.

Brittanys are ideal for active families who want a sporting breed without giant-breed size. They're wonderful with children, friendly with everyone, and remarkably trainable. Their sensitivity makes positive training methods essential.

This breed needs serious exercise — more than most medium-sized dogs. An underexercised Brittany is a hyperactive, destructive one. With adequate activity, they're calm, affectionate indoor companions.
history: Named after the Brittany region of France. Developed in the 1800s as a versatile hunting dog. Originally called 'Brittany Spaniels' but the AKC dropped 'Spaniel' in 1982 because the breed points more than it flushes. One of the most popular field trial breeds. AKC recognised in 1934.
fun_facts: The AKC dropped 'Spaniel' from the name because Brittanys point like setters, not flush like spaniels.
They're one of the most versatile sporting breeds — point, flush, and retrieve.
Brittanys are consistently top performers in field trials and hunt tests.
Despite being gun dogs, they're among the most sensitive sporting breeds.
puppy_tips: Provide serious exercise from the start. Brittany puppies have enormous energy.
Use only gentle, positive training methods. Brittanys are extremely sensitive to correction.
Start socialisation early. They're naturally friendly but benefit from broad exposure.
Channel their natural pointing and retrieving instincts into games.
Mental stimulation is as important as physical exercise. Puzzle toys, scent work, and varied training.
diet_notes: High-quality performance nutrition for this athletic breed. Higher protein and fat for active individuals. Feed 2 meals daily.
exercise_notes: 90-150 minutes daily of vigorous exercise. Running, swimming, field work, hiking. This breed needs to RUN — walks alone are insufficient. Excellent for runners and outdoor enthusiasts.
grooming_notes: Low to moderate maintenance. Brush 1-2x weekly. Feathering on legs and ears needs attention. Clean ears regularly. Professional grooming optional.

name: Cocker Spaniel
coat_type: Double coat, medium to long, silky with feathering
coat_colors: ASCOB (Any Solid Color Other than Black)
training_tips: Always positive training. Cockers are gentle and wilt under harsh methods.
Clean ears religiously — at least weekly. Ear infections are the #1 health issue.
Build confidence. Some Cockers are submissive urinators — ignore accidents and reward confidence.
Make grooming a positive experience from puppyhood. It's a lifelong major commitment.
Socialise broadly. Cockers are naturally friendly and benefit from varied positive experiences.
recommended_training_style: Gentle, positive, confidence-building. Never harsh. Make training joyful.

common_conditions.description: Long, heavy ears trap moisture and debris.
Immune system attacks red blood cells.

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The American Cocker Spaniel is the breed that put 'merry' in merry temperament. With their soulful eyes, silky ears, and perpetual tail wag, Cockers are one of the most beloved family dogs in history.

Cockers are gentle, adaptable companions equally happy on a walk or curled up on the sofa. Their sporting heritage gives them enough energy for active play without the demanding exercise needs of larger sporting breeds. They're wonderful with children and other pets.

The breed's biggest practical challenge is grooming. That gorgeous coat requires daily maintenance and professional grooming. Ear infections are a constant battle due to the heavy, pendulous ears. With proper care, a Cocker's beautiful appearance matches their beautiful temperament.
history: Developed from English Cocker Spaniels in America. The name 'Cocker' comes from their original job flushing woodcock. American breeders selected for a smaller, more glamorous companion dog compared to the working English type. AKC recognised in 1878. Lady from Lady and the Tramp is a Cocker.
fun_facts: The name 'Cocker' comes from flushing woodcock — a type of bird.
Lady from Disney's Lady and the Tramp is an American Cocker Spaniel.
They were the #1 AKC breed from 1936-1952.
President Nixon's Cocker Spaniel, Checkers, was famous from a pivotal TV speech.
puppy_tips: Start ear care immediately. Clean ears at least weekly — infections are virtually guaranteed without prevention.
Begin grooming routines from day one. Daily brushing and monthly professional grooming are lifelong.
Use positive training only. Cockers are sensitive and shut down with harsh methods.
Build confidence. Some Cockers are naturally submissive — reward brave behaviour generously.
Socialise with dogs and people early. Cockers are naturally friendly and should stay that way.
diet_notes: High-quality food, potentially limited-ingredient if allergies develop. Watch weight. Ear health can be influenced by diet — omega-3 supplements may help.
exercise_notes: 45-75 minutes daily. Moderate walks, play, and retrieval games. Not extreme exercise but need daily activity. Enjoy swim but dry ears thoroughly afterwards.
grooming_notes: High-maintenance grooming. Daily brushing essential. Professional grooming every 4-6 weeks. Many owners opt for a shorter sport clip. Ear cleaning weekly minimum. Regular dental care.

name: Miniature American Shepherd
common_training_challenges: Herding instinct toward children and pets
training_tips: Treat as a small Australian Shepherd — same intelligence, same needs, smaller package.
Socialise with strangers early. MAS can be reserved without positive exposure.
Provide mental challenges daily. Puzzle toys, trick training, agility.
Redirect herding instinct into structured activities.
Build independence to prevent excessive attachment.
recommended_training_style: Challenge their intelligence. Positive, varied, activity-rich training.

common_conditions.description: Drug sensitivity common in herding breeds.

medication_sensitivities: Certain anaesthetics — inform vet of MDR1 status
spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (6-12 months).
breed_description:
The Miniature American Shepherd is essentially a compact Australian Shepherd — same intelligence, same versatility, same work ethic, smaller package. Developed for owners who wanted Aussie brains in a more portable size.

MAS are brilliant, devoted, and eager to work. They excel at agility, obedience, and trick training. Their smaller size makes them more adaptable to apartment living, though their energy and mental needs are just as demanding as their larger cousins.

Like all herding breeds, they need a job. Without mental stimulation, they'll create their own entertainment — and it won't be what you had in mind.
history: Developed in California in the 1960s from small Australian Shepherds. Originally called 'Miniature Australian Shepherds' until the AKC granted separate breed recognition. Popular with rodeo riders who wanted a smart travel companion. AKC recognised in 2015.
fun_facts: MAS were originally just small Australian Shepherds selectively bred down in size.
They're one of the newest AKC-recognised breeds (2015).
Popular with equestrians and rodeo riders as travel companions.
They can have heterochromia (two different-coloured eyes) like Australian Shepherds.
puppy_tips: Provide daily mental challenges from puppyhood. This breed is too smart for idle time.
Socialise with strangers early and often. MAS can be reserved or shy without exposure.
Channel herding instinct into dog sports. Agility, flyball, and obedience are ideal.
Test for MDR1 gene immediately. This affects medication safety.
Build independence. MAS bond deeply and can develop separation anxiety.
diet_notes: Standard small breed diet. Adjust portions to activity level. Quality protein for active dogs.
exercise_notes: 60-90 minutes daily of active exercise and mental stimulation. Agility, hiking, trick training. Need both physical and mental outlets. More demanding than typical small breeds.
grooming_notes: Moderate to high shedding. Brush 2x weekly, daily during coat blows. Never shave double coat. Regular ear cleaning. Professional grooming optional.

name: Border Collie
coat_type: Double coat, rough or smooth variety, medium length
common_training_challenges: THE most intelligent breed — they outsmart their owners regularly
Can develop anxiety without adequate stimulation
training_tips: A Border Collie without a job is a Border Collie with a problem. Provide structured mental work EVERY DAY.
This is the smartest breed on Earth. They will learn your patterns, predict your behaviour, and manipulate routines.
Channel herding instinct into sanctioned activities: agility, herding trials, flyball, disc.
Watch for obsessive behaviours. Ball fixation, shadow chasing, and light chasing are common and harmful.
Socialise with calm confidence. Border Collies can be nervous or reactive without careful exposure.
recommended_training_style: Challenge constantly. This breed needs to THINK. Structured, varied, mentally demanding training.
medication_sensitivities: Certain anaesthetics — inform vet of MDR1 status
spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing (12-18 months recommended).
breed_description:
The Border Collie is, by virtually every measure, the most intelligent dog breed on Earth. Bred on the Scottish-English border for sheep herding, Border Collies combine laser-like focus, tireless energy, and problem-solving ability that can be either awe-inspiring or exhausting.

This is NOT a breed for casual dog owners. Border Collies need hours of daily mental and physical stimulation. Without it, they develop anxiety, obsessive behaviours, and destructive habits. They will herd your children, outsmart your gates, and find ways to entertain themselves that you won't appreciate.

In the right home — with an active, engaged owner who provides structured work — a Border Collie is the most rewarding dog you'll ever own. They learn anything, excel at everything, and their devotion is absolute.
history: Developed along the Anglo-Scottish border for sheep herding. The breed's defining ancestor is a dog named Old Hemp, born in 1893, who was so good at herding that he sired over 200 puppies. The 'eye' — an intense stare used to control sheep — is the breed's signature. AKC recognised in 1995.
fun_facts: Border Collies are the most intelligent dog breed, capable of learning 1,000+ words.
Chaser, a Border Collie, could identify 1,022 toys by name — the largest vocabulary of any non-human animal.
The breed's characteristic 'eye' stare is so intense it can control sheep from 100 metres away.
They can run up to 30 mph and commonly clock 40+ miles per day working sheep.
celebrity_dogs: Rico (studied by researchers for word comprehension)
Striker (fastest car window opened by a dog — Guinness Record)
puppy_tips: Provide structured mental work from day one. This isn't optional — it's survival.

breed_description:

Weimaraners are high-energy, intelligent dogs who need experienced owners committed to extensive exercise and training. In the right home, they're loyal, affectionate companions. In the wrong home, they're anxious, destructive tornadoes.

As puppies, they're adorable but demanding — expect separation anxiety, boundless energy, and impressive counter-surfing skills.

history: Chihuahuas likely descended from the Techichi, a small companion dog kept by the Toltec civilisation in Mexico. The modern breed was discovered in the Mexican state of Chihuahua in the 1850s and brought to the United States. The AKC recognised them in 1904.

Bassets are gentle, patient, and excellent family dogs. They're wonderful with children, friendly with other dogs, and content with moderate exercise. Their stubbornness is legendary but part of their charm.

Keep towels everywhere for the drool
diet_notes: 3-4 cups of high-quality food daily, split into 2-3 meals. Feed from elevated bowls. No exercise within an hour of eating — bloat prevention is critical. Consider a slow-feeder bowl.
exercise_notes: 45-60 minutes daily. They need walks where they can sniff — it's their primary mental stimulation. Avoid off-leash in unfenced areas — once on a scent, they're gone.
grooming_notes: Weekly brushing with a hound glove. Clean wrinkles and facial folds daily. Clean ears weekly to prevent infections. Embrace the drool — it's part of the package.

name: Akita
common_training_challenges: Can be aggressive with other dogs
Not eager to please in the way retrievers are
training_tips: Establish yourself as a confident leader early
Socialize extensively with dogs and people
Use firm but fair training — never harsh punishment
Short, purposeful training sessions work best
Consider professional training classes for this breed
recommended_training_style: Confident leadership, firm boundaries, reward-based but structured. Best for experienced owners
spay_neuter_recommendation: Wait until 18-24 months for proper joint and growth plate development
breed_description: The Akita is a powerful, noble breed that embodies loyalty and courage. Originally bred for guarding Japanese royalty and hunting large game, they form intensely devoted bonds with their family while remaining aloof with strangers. They're quiet, clean dogs who demand an experienced owner who understands their complex personality.
history: Ancient Japanese breed from the mountainous northern regions. Declared a Natural Monument of Japan in 1931. Hachiko, the world's most famous Akita, waited for his deceased owner at a train station for nine years.
fun_facts: Helen Keller brought the first Akita to America in 1937
Hachiko is Japan's most beloved dog — a statue stands at Shibuya Station
In Japan, figurines of Akitas are given as gifts to wish good health
Originally used to hunt bears, boar, and deer
puppy_tips: Socialize, socialize, socialize — this breed needs it more than most
Start training early while they're still manageable in size
Teach children to respect the puppy's space
Get them used to grooming early — lots of coat to manage
Monitor interactions with other dogs carefully
diet_notes: 3-5 cups of high-quality food daily depending on size and activity. Feed twice daily. Prone to bloat — avoid vigorous exercise after meals. Some Akitas do well on fish-based diets.
exercise_notes: 45-75 minutes daily of moderate exercise. They enjoy walks and can be jogging partners. Avoid extreme heat — their thick coat makes them prone to overheating.
grooming_notes: Brush 2-3 times weekly, daily during heavy shedding (twice yearly 'coat blow'). They're naturally clean dogs — cat-like in grooming habits. Bathe occasionally.

name: Portuguese Water Dog
coat_type: Wavy or curly, single coat, non-shedding
common_training_challenges: Needs lots of activity or becomes destructive
Separation anxiety if left alone too much
training_tips: Channel their intelligence into complex tricks and tasks
Swimming is the ultimate exercise and reward for them
Provide plenty of interactive toys and puzzles
Crate train early to prevent separation anxiety
They excel at dock diving and water sports
recommended_training_style: Enthusiastic positive reinforcement, activity-based rewards, complex trick training to challenge their intelligence

common_conditions.description: Heart muscle disease in young dogs

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing at 6-12 months; discuss with vet
breed_description: The Portuguese Water Dog is a spirited, athletic breed with boundless enthusiasm for life — especially if water is involved. Hypoallergenic and highly intelligent, they make wonderful family dogs for active households. Their curly coat, webbed feet, and love of swimming make them unique among working breeds.
history: Bred along the Portuguese coast to herd fish into nets, retrieve lost tackle, and act as couriers between ships. Their swimming ability is legendary. Gained popularity after becoming the Obama family's White House dog.
fun_facts: Have webbed feet for swimming
Bo and Sunny were the Obama family's Portuguese Water Dogs
Can dive underwater to retrieve objects
Were nearly extinct in the 1930s — saved by a wealthy shipping magnate
puppy_tips: Introduce water early and safely — they'll become obsessed
Start grooming routine immediately — their coat requires constant maintenance
Provide LOTS of exercise — a tired Portie is a good Portie
Teach them not to jump early — they're bouncy
Interactive toys prevent separation anxiety when you leave
diet_notes: High-quality, protein-rich food to support their athletic build. About 2.5-3.5 cups daily. They're food-motivated — great for training but watch the treat calories.
exercise_notes: 60-90 minutes of vigorous daily exercise. Swimming is ideal. Also enjoy fetch, agility, and dock diving. Without sufficient exercise, they'll find their own (destructive) entertainment.
grooming_notes: Brush every other day to prevent matting. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Two clip styles: retriever clip (even length) or lion clip (traditional). Non-shedding but high-maintenance coat.

name: Chesapeake Bay Retriever
coat_type: Thick double coat, oily and waterproof
training_tips: Give tasks meaning — they need to understand 'why'
Water retrieve games are the ultimate reward
Socialize well to temper protective instincts
Positive but firm training works best
They're more serious than Labs — respect their intensity
recommended_training_style: Purposeful, reward-based with clear objectives. They work best when they see the point of what you're asking

common_conditions.description: Episodes of weakness/collapse during intense exercise

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 18-24 months for proper joint development
breed_description: The Chesapeake Bay Retriever is the toughest of the retriever breeds — bred to work the icy waters of Chesapeake Bay retrieving waterfowl. More independent and protective than their Lab and Golden cousins, Chessies form deep bonds with their family and are tireless workers with hearts of gold.
history: Descended from two Newfoundland puppies rescued from a shipwreck in Maryland in 1807. Crossed with local retrievers to create a breed tough enough for the rough, cold waters of Chesapeake Bay.
fun_facts: The state dog of Maryland
Their oily coat repels water like a duck's feathers
Can break through ice to retrieve waterfowl
One of few breeds truly 'Made in America'
puppy_tips: Introduce water early — it's in their DNA
Socialize extensively as they can be wary of strangers
Channel their retrieving instinct into structured games
Start obedience early — they'll challenge authority in adolescence
Provide weather-resistant toys — they love playing in any conditions
diet_notes: 3-4 cups of high-quality food daily. Their active lifestyle requires good nutrition. Feed twice daily. Athletic build should be maintained — lean, not heavy.
exercise_notes: 60-90 minutes of vigorous exercise daily. Swimming and retrieval games are ideal. They thrive in outdoor activities — hiking, hunting, dock diving.
grooming_notes: Minimal grooming — brush 1-2 times weekly. Their oily, waterproof coat shouldn't be over-bathed or it loses its water resistance. Dry them after swimming to prevent hot spots.

name: Dalmatian
common_training_challenges: Extremely high energy — destructive if bored
Some can be nippy as puppies
training_tips: Exercise before training sessions — they focus better when not wired
Use positive methods — they have excellent memories for bad experiences
Channel their endurance into running or cycling alongside you
Teach calm behaviors and impulse control early
They excel at agility and obedience competitions
recommended_training_style: Energetic positive reinforcement, activity-based rewards, consistent with variety to prevent boredom

common_conditions.description: Unique uric acid metabolism predisposes to urate stones

spay_neuter_recommendation: Standard timing; discuss with vet. BAER testing should be done first to identify hearing issues
breed_description: The Dalmatian is an athletic, distinctive breed whose spotted coat makes them one of the most recognizable dogs in the world. Originally bred as carriage dogs, they have incredible endurance and energy. Beneath their striking appearance is an intelligent, loyal companion who thrives on activity and human connection.
history: Named for Dalmatia (modern Croatia), they served as carriage dogs, running alongside horse-drawn coaches. Later became firehouse mascots, running with horse-drawn fire engines. Their endurance is legendary.
fun_facts: Dalmatian puppies are born pure white — spots develop at 2-4 weeks
No two Dalmatians have the same spot pattern
Historical firehouse dogs — they calmed the horses and guarded equipment
Have unique uric acid metabolism unlike any other breed
puppy_tips: Get BAER hearing test done early — 8% are born deaf
Start exercise routines early — they need A LOT of activity
Ensure plenty of water — their unique metabolism requires it
Socialize extensively during puppyhood
Be patient with house training — they can be slow to catch on
diet_notes: Special dietary consideration — Dalmatians have a unique purine metabolism. Feed low-purine foods, avoid organ meats. Ensure constant access to fresh water. 2.5-3.5 cups daily.
exercise_notes: 90-120 minutes of vigorous exercise daily. Born to run — they're perfect jogging or cycling partners. Without adequate exercise, destructive behavior is almost guaranteed.
grooming_notes: Short coat but heavy shedders — the white hairs get everywhere. Brush 1-2 times weekly with a rubber curry brush. Bathe as needed. Short coat means they feel temperature extremes.

name: Samoyed
coat_type: Thick double coat, profuse and weather-resistant
common_training_challenges: Separation anxiety if left alone often
training_tips: Use their social nature as motivation — they want to be with you
Teach 'quiet' early — Samoyeds are vocal dogs
Cold weather training is ideal — they thrive in it
Provide outlets for their working dog energy
Make training feel like play — they respond to fun
recommended_training_style: Playful positive reinforcement, social motivation, cold-weather activities when possible
spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 12-18 months for proper development
breed_description: The Samoyed is a cloud-white bundle of joy known for their perpetual smile and sunny disposition. Bred by the Samoyede people of Siberia as sled dogs and companions, they literally slept with their families for warmth — creating one of the most people-oriented breeds. Their 'Sammy smile' isn't just cute — the upturned corners of their mouth prevent drooling in freezing temperatures.
history: Bred by the Samoyede people of Siberia for herding reindeer, pulling sleds, and keeping families warm. They lived intimately with their humans, sleeping in tents together — creating their deep bond with people.
fun_facts: Their 'Sammy smile' prevents drooling that would freeze on their face in cold weather
Their undercoat can be spun into yarn — it's called 'Sammie fur' or 'chiengora'
Used in polar expeditions by Amundsen and Scott
One of the oldest dog breeds — 3,000+ year heritage
celebrity_dogs: Etah (led Amundsen to the South Pole)
puppy_tips: Start grooming from day one — they need DAILY brushing as adults
Teach them to enjoy alone time early to prevent separation anxiety
Provide plenty of social interaction — they're pack animals
Keep them cool in summer — they overheat easily
Redirect chewing to appropriate items — their mouthy phase is long
diet_notes: 2-3 cups of high-quality food daily. Their metabolism is efficient — they don't need as much food as you'd expect for their size. Monitor weight as their fluff can hide weight gain.
exercise_notes: 60-90 minutes of exercise daily. They love pulling activities, hiking, and cold-weather adventures. Avoid heavy exercise in heat — walk early morning or evening in summer.
grooming_notes: Daily brushing is non-negotiable. Professional grooming every 6-8 weeks. Heavy shedding ('coat blowing') twice yearly. NEVER shave a Samoyed — their coat regulates temperature in both hot and cold.

name: Australian Cattle Dog
common_training_challenges: Nipping and herding instinct with people
training_tips: Give them a job — they need purpose
Channel herding instincts into structured activities
Teach 'no nip' early — they'll try to herd everything
Agility and advanced obedience are perfect outlets
Rotate activities to keep their brilliant minds engaged
recommended_training_style: Task-oriented, intellectually challenging, varied activities. They need a purpose — not just obedience but real work

common_conditions.description: Congenital hearing loss linked to coat color
Cartilage and bone condition in growing dogs

spay_neuter_recommendation: Consider waiting until 12-18 months for active breed joint development
breed_description: The Australian Cattle Dog (also called Blue Heeler or Red Heeler) is a relentlessly energetic, razor-sharp working dog bred for the harsh Australian outback. They form velcro-like bonds with their person and need a job to be happy. In the right home, they're extraordinarily loyal, intelligent, and capable — the ultimate active companion.
history: Developed in 19th century Australia by crossing native Dingoes with imported herding dogs (including Collies and Dalmatians) to create a tough cattle herder that could handle Australia's vast, harsh landscape.
fun_facts: Bluey, an Australian Cattle Dog, holds the record for the oldest dog ever — 29 years and 5 months
Have Dingo DNA in their ancestry
Born white and develop their color pattern over weeks
One of the smartest dog breeds — ranked #10 in intelligence
puppy_tips: Redirect nipping from day one — they WILL try to herd your ankles
Provide mental challenges daily — puzzle feeders, hide-and-seek, trick training
Socialize with many different people to prevent over-bonding with just you
Start with structured activities early — they need routine and purpose
Expect a very mouthy puppy — provide lots of appropriate chew outlets
diet_notes: 2-3 cups of high-quality, high-protein food daily. Active dogs need more calories. Monitor weight — they should be lean and muscular, not bulky.
exercise_notes: 90-120 minutes of intense exercise daily — minimum. Herding games, agility, frisbee, running, and trick training. Mental exercise is equally important. An under-exercised ACD is a destructive ACD.
grooming_notes: Low maintenance — weekly brushing with a bristle brush. Heavier shedding twice yearly. Bathe only when needed. Check ears regularly. Their tough coat handles outdoor conditions well.

name: Mixed Breed
common_training_challenges: Each mix is unique — observe and adapt
May have inherited traits from multiple breeds
Background may be unknown — watch for unexpected behaviors
Some rescue mixes may have past trauma to work through
training_tips: Observe what motivates YOUR dog — food, toys, praise, or play
Watch for breed-specific behaviors that hint at their mix
Positive reinforcement works for all dogs regardless of breed
Be flexible — adapt your approach to what works for your individual dog
DNA tests can reveal breed mix and potential health concerns
recommended_training_style: Flexible, positive reinforcement adapted to the individual dog's personality and motivations

common_conditions.description: Mixed breeds benefit from 'hybrid vigor' but can inherit conditions from parent breeds
Common across all dogs, especially smaller mixes
Common when diet isn't matched to size and activity level

spay_neuter_recommendation: Discuss timing with your vet based on estimated adult size
breed_description: Mixed breed dogs are beautifully unique — no two are exactly alike. Whether intentionally crossed or delightfully random, mixed breeds often benefit from 'hybrid vigor,' potentially reducing the risk of breed-specific health issues. Your mixed breed puppy is one of a kind, and their personality will unfold in wonderful, surprising ways as they grow.
history: Mixed breed dogs have existed as long as dogs themselves. While purebreds are relatively modern inventions (most breeds standardized in the 1800s), mixed breeds represent the natural diversity of the canine species. Today, they're the most popular type of dog in America.
fun_facts: Mixed breeds make up the majority of dogs in shelters and homes worldwide
Often called 'mutts' with pride — they're genetically diverse
DNA tests can reveal surprising ancestry — many 'mutts' have unexpected breed combinations
Studies suggest mixed breeds may live longer on average than purebreds
celebrity_dogs: Old Yeller (was a mixed breed in the book)
Toto (actually a Cairn Terrier, but mixed breeds are the real stars!)
puppy_tips: Get a DNA test to understand potential breed traits and health risks
Observe your puppy's natural behaviors to guide your training approach
Estimate adult size based on paw size and DNA results for food planning
Socialize extensively — you want to cover all bases with unknown backgrounds
Celebrate their uniqueness — there's literally no other dog like yours in the world
diet_notes: Choose food appropriate for their estimated adult size (small, medium, or large breed formula). Monitor growth rate and adjust portions. About 1-4 cups daily depending on size.
exercise_notes: Depends on size and energy level. Start with 30-60 minutes daily and adjust based on your dog's needs. Watch for signs of over- or under-exercise. Variety is key — mix walks, play, and mental games.
grooming_notes: Depends entirely on coat type. Short coats need weekly brushing, longer or curly coats need more frequent grooming. Start grooming routines early regardless of coat type to build comfort.
